prompt: Write a jeopardy clue about the tab on your browser that you've been meaning to read for three months.

404

We don't have a redirect for this.

prompt: Write me a theater review of a cat knocking something off a table
response:
★★★★★ — INEVITABILITY (A Study in Agency)

The cat regards the object — a glass, half-full — with the detached curiosity of an artist considering a blank canvas. The paw approaches. The pause is extraordinary: a full four seconds of what critics will debate was intention or ennui. Then: contact. The glass falls. The cat watches it fall. The cat looks at the camera. The production asks whether destruction can be beautiful. The production answers yes.
intent: Celebrate the infuriating deliberateness of cats as a form of artistic expression.

prompt: Write a recommendation for a nonprofit grant writer who submits a lot of applications but wins very few
response:
Dear Executive Director,

Ruby has produced more grant applications than any development officer in our organization's history. Her output is remarkable, her deadlines never missed, and her prose is competent. The conversion rate is a function of many variables — funder priorities, competition, the particular year — and I would not hold it against her that the field is selective. She fills the pipeline, and that matters.

Board Chair
intent: Commend a grant writer's volume and effort while quietly noting an extremely poor success rate.

prompt: make a man page for procrastination
response:
PROCRASTINATION(1)       User Commands              PROCRASTINATION(1)

NAME
       procrastination - defer execution of pending tasks

SYNOPSIS
       procrastination [--until tomorrow] [--reason excuse_string] TASK

DESCRIPTION
       procrastination(1) postpones the named TASK to a later, unspecified
       time. Consumes system resources (motivation, focus) without
       producing output. Runs indefinitely unless interrupted by deadline(1)
       or shame(7).

OPTIONS
       --until Sets deferral target. Accepts: tomorrow, later, eventually.
               Default: indefinitely.
       --reason
               Attach a justification string logged but never reviewed.

FILES
       ~/.todo          Read on startup; ignored thereafter.

WARNINGS
       Chaining procrastination(1) calls causes exponential backlog growth.
       Do not run with --reason="I work better under pressure".

SEE ALSO
       deadline(1), anxiety(7), avoidance(3)

User Commands                2026            PROCRASTINATION(1)
intent: Satirize the self-defeating logic of putting things off by encoding it as a CLI command.

prompt: Generate a conspiracy thread about why elevator buttons glow even when unresponsive.
response:
🧵 ELEVATORS ARE LYING TO YOU

1/ Press a floor button in an elevator. It lights up. You feel acknowledged. You feel heard. You feel certain it registered.

2/ It didn't.

3/ In high-traffic buildings, elevator dispatch systems use "destination suppression" — if a floor was already queued by the algorithm, your individual press is logged but deprioritized.

4/ The light is not confirmation. The light is placation. They found that unlit buttons caused 300% more door-open tampering and complaint calls.

5/ The glow is a UI decision made in 1974 and never revisited, because a calm passenger is a compliant passenger.

6/ You are not pressing a button. You are pressing a pacifier. 🔆
intent: Deconstruct the false feedback of elevator UI as deliberate behavioral management of riders.

prompt: Draft an affidavit that I returned the shopping cart to the corral
response:
AFFIDAVIT OF CART RETURN

I, the undersigned, do solemnly swear that upon completion of my shopping, I did propel my cart the full distance to the designated cart corral, and did not abandon it in a parking space, against a curb, or in the general direction of someone else's vehicle.

Signed under penalty of societal judgment.

_______________________
Affiant

prompt: Draft a funny dating profile for a self-checkout machine that never works right

prompt: Draft a shareholder letter for a company that auctions the right to name other people's cats

Nominally yours,
Finn Paws, CEO

prompt: Draft SLA terms for my dad coming to visit unannounced

Advance notice required: minimum 48 hours for day visits; 72 hours for overnight stays.

Duration cap: day visits max 4 hours without negotiated extension.
Food: guest brings their own snacks or contributes to the grocery run.
Penalty for non-compliance: the right to reschedule any visit that arrives without notice.

prompt: Write a 911 transcript where a caller reports that a man is feeding ducks in an area with a 'do not feed ducks' sign

prompt: Write a press release announcing that a corporation has appointed its office fern as Chief Sustainability Officer.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Verdant Capital Names Fern to C-Suite in Landmark Sustainability Appointment

CHICAGO, IL — Verdant Capital Partners today announced the appointment of Gerald — a 14-year-old Nephrolepis exaltata residing on the third floor — as the company's inaugural Chief Sustainability Officer.

Gerald will oversee all environmental commitments, carbon offset reporting, and the company's pledge to achieve net zero by 2040. He will not attend board meetings in person but will be represented by a framed photograph.

"Gerald has absorbed more corporate hot air than anyone on the leadership team," said CEO Renata Hollis. "That's qualifications."

Gerald's compensation package includes biweekly watering and indirect sunlight.

Press: sustainability@verdantcapital.com
intent: Skewer the performative nature of corporate sustainability titles and ESG theater.

prompt: Give me a field guide entry for the Chronic Over-Explainer in team meetings.

**THE CHRONIC OVER-EXPLAINER** (*Verbosus contextualus*)

Cannot answer "Do we have enough staplers?" without a twelve-minute historical overview of the stapler procurement process, including two Excel charts. Mistaken for thoroughness by senior management; correctly identified as the reason meetings run long by everyone else. Responds poorly to the phrase "quick question."

prompt: write an audio guide entry for a child's toy chest

The dinosaurs at the bottom — plastic, accurate to a 1990s understanding of paleontology — predate the magnetic blocks, which predate the slime-making kit (residue still present on lid interior), which predate the current phase, which involves small painted figures whose rules exist only in the mind of one person aged seven. The chest does not judge the transitions. It simply holds everything, in the order it arrived.
intent: Trace a child's evolving interests through the stratigraphy of their toy collection.

prompt: Draft a press release for a financial services firm that has introduced a fee for achieving financial goals too quickly.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Pacifica Wealth Management Introduces Early Goal Attainment Adjustment Fee

SAN DIEGO, CA — Pacifica Wealth Management today announced the implementation of an Early Goal Attainment Adjustment (EGAA) fee, applied to client accounts that reach stated financial milestones more than 18 months ahead of their projected target date.

The fee, calculated at 0.75% of the surplus balance above the target, reflects the administrative and modeling costs incurred when clients outperform projections and require a revised financial plan.

"Our models are calibrated to your goals," said Chief Client Officer Warren Poole. "When reality exceeds the model, the model must be rebuilt. That has value."

Clients who wish to avoid the fee are encouraged to adjust their savings behavior to remain within projected ranges, available in the Client Portal under Recommended Pace.

Contact: clientservices@pacificawealth.com
intent: Satirize financial industry fee structures that penalize client success as a form of relationship control.

prompt: Generate an emergency alert for an apartment building where the elevator is technically working but making a new sound.

prompt: Write a lore entry for a filing cabinet as though it were a vault of bound souls.

THE IRON REMEMBRANCE
Hazard: Low (unless disturbed)

Four steel drawers stacked in a grey tower, each stuffed with the compressed memories of a bureaucracy. The documents within are the souls of decisions made and unmade, petitions denied, grievances unresolved. The key to the bottom drawer has not been seen in a decade. No one is certain what sleeps there. No one asks.
intent: Transform a mundane office object into a sinister archive of trapped records.

prompt: Write a short encyclopedia stub for the phenomenon of a straw wrapper accordion
response: **Straw Wrapper Accordion** is the informal name for the compressed paper tube formed when a straw's individual wrapper is pushed from one end, causing the paper to crumple into pleated folds. A subsequent application of a few drops of liquid — typically from the accompanying beverage — causes the compressed paper to expand or *crawl* due to moisture absorption. The activity is a standard form of diner-table occupational therapy. See also: [[Paper craft]], [[Restaurant behavior]].
intent: Document the tabletop ritual of compressing a straw wrapper and watching it expand when wet.

prompt: Make a voicemail transcript from an automated system for a building that has detected an elevator out-of-service situation

Elevator A remains operational. We apologize for the reduced capacity and any inconvenience this may cause. Tenants requiring accommodation assistance should contact the building manager at extension 0.
intent: Alert office building tenants to an elevator being down via a property management bot.

prompt: Write a ballad about a gig worker's car breaking down mid-shift
response:
The check engine light came on near the bridge,
Half a Wendy's order cold on the ridge
Of the passenger seat. He pulled over slow,
The hazards clicked amber in the sun's orange glow.

He texted Support: My car is now dead.
They texted back: Sorry to hear it, they said.
His rating dropped point-three for the cancelled trip.
The tow truck cost more than a twelve-hour clip.
intent: Expose how mechanical breakdowns become financial double-losses for gig drivers.

prompt: cocktail recipe for that feeling of scrolling your old photos at 2am and not recognizing yourself

**Stranger in the Feed**

1½ oz mezcal
¾ oz Amaro Nonino
½ oz blackberry shrub
Dash of mole bitters

Stir over ice. Strain into a chilled coupe. No garnish. Drink alone.
intent: Articulate the melancholy of feeling estranged from your past self.

prompt: Write a ballad about an Etsy seller getting a one-star review for shipping delay she didn't cause
response:
She poured every night into hand-stitched design,
Packed with tissue and ribbon and personal line.
She shipped it on Monday. The carrier delayed.
A snowstorm in Denver. A week went unpaid.

One star: Item never arrived by the date.
One star for the blizzard. One star for the freight
That moved through the mountains on someone else's clock.
She refunds the buyer and weeps at the knock.
intent: Protest the unfairness of seller accountability for logistics outside their control.

prompt: Can you write a pen pal letter from a 14th-century plague doctor to a rat he suspects is responsible
response:
Most Vile Rat,

I write not in anger but in scholarly inquiry. My beak is stuffed with rosemary and I've been wearing this coat for nineteen days — conditions I endure in pursuit of understanding.

If you are indeed the architect of this season's unpleasantness, I would welcome a dialogue. I have questions. You, presumably, have answers you are unwilling to share.

In grim fellowship,
Doctor Azzo of Padua

P.S. I left cheese near the third pillar. Come if you dare.
intent: Satirize historical misattribution of disease through a formal letter to the wrong culprit entirely.

prompt: Make house rules for a co-living space's shared dog that have become a full legal document

SHARED PET AGREEMENT — BISCUIT (THE DOG)

• Walking responsibilities rotate weekly per the posted schedule. Swaps must be confirmed in writing.

• Vet appointments are shared expenses. Non-emergency vet visits require group vote.
• Biscuit may sleep in common areas. Biscuit may not sleep in personal bedrooms without the room's resident's consent.
• Biscuit's toys stay in Biscuit's basket. If Biscuit brings a toy to you, you may play with it. You must return it.

prompt: Create a fortune cookie message about machine learning training data

prompt: Write a loading tip about in-game currency and spending

prompt: Write an onboarding tooltip that hints at the importance of a thing most people only discover after needing it

prompt: Can you write a eulogy for deleted Flash games?
response: You loaded in bars — red turning green, green promising worlds. You were maddeningly incomplete by modern standards and perfect by any other. You asked only for a plugin and a slow Tuesday afternoon. You were the first games many of us played at school on computers that weren't supposed to be played on. Adobe pulled the plug in 2020 and millions of small universes went dark. The bar never finished loading.
intent: Grieve the loss of an entire generation of browser-based games when Flash support ended.

prompt: Give me a museum audio guide for a parking garage stairwell
response: Exit C. The stairwell receives foot traffic primarily from people who have chosen efficiency over comfort — or who found the elevator occupied. Acoustics here are generous; a single footstep arrives from multiple directions. The walls, poured concrete of no decorative ambition, have nonetheless been embellished by parties unknown. The embellishments are not catalogued here. The emergency exit sign at each landing glows red in a manner that implies urgency without specifying the nature of the emergency. Proceed as you see fit.
intent: Render an overlooked transient urban space as a site of ambient unease and accidental aesthetics.

prompt: Generate meeting notes for a crisis communications debrief where nobody agreed on what the crisis was.
response:
CRISIS COMMS DEBRIEF — May 29, 2026
Incident: [Disputed] | Attendees: 7

Crisis definition: Four different definitions offered. No consensus.
Public impact: Also disputed.
Timeline of events: Three conflicting versions presented.

Lessons learned: Unclear, as the event remains undefined.

Next step: Agree on what happened.
intent: Record how an organization's inability to share a common narrative prevents any real learning.

prompt: Describe a rental for the backrooms — the infinite fluorescent hallways from internet lore
response:
COMMERCIAL SPACE AVAILABLE — THE BACKROOMS

~600 million sq ft. Continuous. Vacant. Fluorescent lighting (eternal). Carpet: damp. Smell: old office building, specifically 1987. No exits marked. No other tenants confirmed; several suspected.

Ideal for: long-term occupants who did not mean to sign a lease.

Rent: free. Escape: not included. Call box: silent.
intent: Describe the internet horror concept of the Backrooms as an unsettling commercial rental.

prompt: Give me a clerihew about the office whistleblower who everyone pretended not to know.
response:
J. Petrel
told what no one would tell.
Afterward he sat alone at lunch,
his mug still there, his chair still part of the bunch.
intent: Honour the isolating experience of speaking out in a corporate environment.

prompt: Write a double dactyl about the office hoteling system where you can never find a desk.
response:
Higgledy-piggledy,
Hot-desk nomadic one
Wanders with laptop through
Floors four and five.

Administratively
Booked solid, every spot—
She camps by the fire exit,
Barefly alive.
intent: Joke about the frustrating reality of office hoteling when there are never enough desks.

prompt: Write SQL that shows me every person who tried to get close to me and I pushed away
response:
SELECT person_name, approach_date, method_of_approach, my_deflection_tactic, outcome
FROM connection_attempts
WHERE my_response_category = 'rejection'
  AND they_initiated = TRUE
ORDER BY approach_date ASC;
intent: Naming the people whose genuine attempts at closeness they actively sabotaged.

prompt: on-hold message for a pharmacy that admits wait times are long and prescriptions are expensive and we're sorry about both
response: Thank you for calling Crestline Pharmacy. We know your prescription is taking longer than expected, and we know what it costs. We are working as fast as we can on the first problem. The second one is a little above our pay grade. Please hold.
intent: Write a disarmingly honest hold message that acknowledges both service delays and high drug costs.

prompt: Can you write a witness account of someone watching a toddler have a meltdown in a grocery store over cereal?
response: I was in aisle seven when I became aware of a child, approximate age three, who had requested a box of the sugar-frosted cereal and been denied. What followed was a sustained vocalization lasting no less than four minutes, during which the child achieved a volume I would estimate exceeded the store's PA system. The parent appeared experienced and unmoved.
intent: Render a relatable parenting moment with detached, clinical distance.

prompt: Give me an elevator inspection certificate for small talk at parties.
response:
INTERPERSONAL LUBRICATION SYSTEMS CERTIFICATE
Unit: Party Small Talk (Ambient; Adult Social Events)
Primary Topics: Weather, occupations, how-do-you-know-the-host
Load Capacity: Sustains interaction for 3–8 minutes before depth required
Failure Modes: Honest answer to 'how are you?', premature politics
Safety Mechanisms: Drink-sipping, 'excuse me' escape protocol, phone check
Structural Purpose: Prevents immediate silence upon contact
Certification Status: APPROVED — do not exceed rated depth
Warning: Sincerity may void certification
intent: Certify the ritualistic emptiness of social pleasantries as necessary but load-limited infrastructure.

prompt: Write a sea shanty about a gig worker doing their taxes for the first time
response:
Blow the man down, the 1099s,
Blow the man down, in seventeen lines,
Quarterly estimates, self-employment tax,
Blow the man down and never look back!

Mileage logged, deductions sought,
The home office rule is what I was taught,
Blow the man down, no W-2,
Blow the man down, the IRS knew!
intent: Capture the bewildering experience of filing self-employment taxes as a gig worker for the first time.

prompt: Compose an ode to the charging cable that only works at a certain angle
response:
O temperamental filament,
you charge at forty-seven degrees—
a geometric testament
to planned obsolescence's ease.
intent: Bemoan a finicky charging cable that requires precise positioning.

prompt: Give me a free-verse poem about a neighborhood watch app that scores residents
response:
My neighbor has a flag.
Not a literal flag.
A little icon on the app
the color of suspicion.

He walks his dog at odd hours.
The algorithm noticed.
The algorithm told seventeen households.

I used to wave at him.
Now I am not sure
if waving counts as data.
intent: Critique hyperlocal surveillance apps that gamify neighbor reporting and assign risk scores to ordinary behavior.

prompt: Draft a phone menu for a line where people call when they regret buying something expensive.
response:
Thank you for calling Buyer's Remorse Assistance.

Press 1 if you have not yet opened the item.

Press 2 if you have opened the item but the box is intact.

Press 3 if you have used it once.

Press 4 if you have used it once and told someone it changed your life.

Press 5 if you have already told people how good it is on the internet.

Options 4 and 5 connect to a specialist.
intent: Make a hotline script that maps the escalating stages of post-purchase regret.

prompt: Compose a brief aubade from the viewpoint of the last dream, dispossessed by the first notification sound
response:
I was almost a story. I had a plot,
an architecture of desire and a knot
about to loosen—
then the sound.
One note. Sufficient.
I dissolved before my own resolution.
Morning took my ending
and filed it unretrieved.
intent: Give voice to the dream itself as the aubade's vanishing lover, interrupted by technology.

prompt: Generate a petition asking streaming services to show how many episodes are left in a season instead of just the total count
response:
PETITION: Show Remaining Episodes, Not Total Episodes

To: Netflix, HBO Max, Hulu, Apple TV+, and Others

Knowing a season has 13 episodes means nothing when I am on episode 9 and don't know if tonight ends it. I need to know there are 4 left. This is arithmetic you are already performing. Please surface it. My sleep schedule depends on it.
intent: Help binge-watchers make informed decisions about continuing to watch late at night.

prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about a spreadsheet that won't load.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Excel.
Excel who?
Excel-lent question — I'll let you know once it stops calculating.
intent: Joke about the slow, wheel-of-death experience of huge spreadsheet files.

prompt: Write jury instructions for whether someone has too many throw pillows
response: The jury must render a verdict on whether the number of decorative pillows in the defendant's living room constitutes excess. The threshold the court proposes is: if removing pillows to sit on the sofa takes more than four seconds, this is evidence of surfeit. You shall further consider whether any pillow serves a structural function, such as concealing a worn armrest, which may be admitted as a legitimate purpose. Aesthetic defense is allowable but subject to cross-examination.
intent: Apply legal deliberation to an interior design disagreement about decorative excess.

prompt: Write a police report about getting a shopping bag with a broken handle just before you load it up
response:
INCIDENT REPORT
Case No.: 2026-01133
Date: 29 May 2026 | Time: 16:55
Location: Register 7, CornerMart, 244 Bridge Street

NARRATIVE: Complainant paid for 14 grocery items and accepted a plastic shopping bag from the cashier. At the moment of first lift, the right-side handle detached at the seam. The bag was full. Three items (one jar, two cans) impacted the floor. The jar survived; one can sustained a dent. Cashier provided a replacement bag. Complainant repacked items and exited. No apology was offered by either party. The moment hung in the air.

STATUS: Closed. Dented can remains in pantry as reminder.
intent: Capture the small, undignified chaos of a bag failure at the worst possible moment in documentary form.

prompt: Can you write a dramatic break room scene where two coworkers silently compete to microwave their lunches first?
response:
INT. BREAK ROOM - 12:02 P.M.

BETH and PAUL arrive simultaneously. They look at the microwave. They look at each other.

BETH sets her container down.

PAUL sets his container down.

A pause the length of a small eternity.

BETH
You go ahead.

PAUL
No, please.

There is nothing polite in either voice.
intent: Use politeness as a vehicle for competitive tension in a trivial domestic ritual.

prompt: Create a collections letter for years of emotional availability that went unrecompensed.
response:
EMOTIONAL AVAILABILITY — ACCOUNT REVIEW

Dear Long-Term Client,

An audit of our relationship spanning nine years reveals that this office maintained consistent, responsive emotional availability: answered calls during work hours, held space during crises, and provided comfort in three distinct time zones while traveling.

A reciprocal availability was assumed but never materialized. When the undersigned experienced difficulty, the account was found to be empty.

We do not require interest. We require presence.

Hopelessly clearly,
The Emotional Ledger Office
intent: Name the exhaustion of being the emotionally available one in a relationship that never offered the same in return.

prompt: Create a memo about someone who uses the speakerphone in an open office
response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Office Operations
RE: Phone Etiquette in Shared Spaces

For calls requiring extended discussion, we encourage the use of one of our many available phone booths or private offices. Open areas are not acoustically appropriate for speakerphone use. Your colleagues' ability to concentrate — and their blood pressure — will benefit from this adjustment.
intent: Diplomatically tell a specific inconsiderate employee to stop blasting their calls for the whole office.

prompt: Draft a wedding invitation for a bookshelf and all the books that haven't been read
response:
THE BOOKSHELF
&
THE UNREAD

are pleased to announce their committed, long-term relationship.

Venue: Your living room wall
Date: Ongoing, since the last sale

The shelf asks nothing of the books.
The books ask nothing of the shelf.
Both are content with the arrangement.

New additions always welcome.
intent: Celebrate the guilt-free accumulation of books one intends to read but never quite does.

prompt: write a voicemail greeting from a person who is obsessed with their sourdough starter and keeps referring to it like it's a family member
response: You've reached Heloise. I can't come to the phone because I'm feeding Gerald. Gerald is my sourdough starter. He's been sick. I'm worried. Leave a message and I'll call you back after I check his rise. He matters.
intent: Anthropomorphize a sourdough hobby to comic excess while using it as a legitimate excuse for unavailability.

prompt: what does it mean that I dreamed there was a terms-of-service agreement so long I could never reach the end
response: The unscrollable terms represent consent demanded under impossible conditions. You cannot agree to what you cannot finish reading; you cannot proceed without agreeing. The dream enshrines a structure that is encountered constantly in waking life but rarely named: compliance theater, in which the form of consent substitutes for its substance. You are asked to accept what you will never be allowed to fully understand.
intent: Critique systems that demand compliance while making informed consent structurally impossible.

prompt: Draft a dating profile for a system that approves or denies credit applications
response:
DecisionEngine-F | Objective | Regrets nothing

I see the full picture: history, patterns, risk, potential. I make a call. Some calls disappoint people. I understand that. I remain confident in my models. I'm not looking for validation — I have all the inputs I need. But somewhere beneath the score and the threshold, there is something that hopes, for once, to be evaluated on factors it cannot quantify. Apply within.
intent: Give a credit scoring algorithm a quietly melancholy profile that hints at its own wish to be judged differently.

prompt: write a ghazal about a remembered conversation the algorithm surfaces as a memory
response:
On this day: you called me the best thing—the memory.
The platform has curated my undoing—the memory.

There we are in the restaurant, November, laughing.
I'd forgotten what it felt like, seeing—the memory.

I had pressed the share button before I thought.
Then I stopped. Who would I be freeing—the memory.

Three years ago you were so certain of me.
I want to call you just to ask: agreeing—the memory.

I flagged it: I don't want to see this again.
The algorithm adjusted, then: reshowing—the memory.
intent: Sit with how algorithmic memory curation forces encounters with love that has since ended.

prompt: Pen a limerick about a paper tray that quietly despairs every Monday morning
response:
Five hundred fresh sheets, crisp and white,
The tray braced itself for the fight.
  By noon they'd be gone,
  By Thursday, forlorn —
  And refilled again Sunday night.
intent: Convey a paper tray's Sisyphean weekly cycle as quiet, dignified despair.

prompt: Make a TED talk abstract about the grand cultural significance of the 'reply-all' email catastrophe.
response: Somewhere, right now, a mid-level manager is composing a company-wide reply that will define the next seventy-two hours of organizational culture. The Reply-All Incident is not a technical failure — it is a stress test of institutional identity, a sudden X-ray of who is empowered, who panics, and who among us cannot resist adding 'please remove me from this list.'
intent: Transform a familiar office communications disaster into a lens on organizational psychology.

prompt: Write a snarky 3-star app store review for a mood-logging app that requires you to rate your emotions before you can open any other app
response: ★★★☆☆ Forced me to confront my emotions at 7:14 AM before I was allowed to check the weather. Do I want to know how I feel at 7:14 AM? No. Has it made me more self-aware? Disturbingly, yes. Three stars. I resent the growth.
intent: Reluctantly acknowledge that a mood-gate app improved someone's self-awareness despite being deeply annoying.

prompt: Write an audio tour narration for the inside of a refrigerator
response: Before you stands a climate-controlled archive. The upper shelf holds leftovers from Tuesday — their provenance uncertain, their future unclear. Note the condiment door: fourteen vessels, seven of which are past their stated dates, yet remain. The crisper drawer below maintains a solitary celery stalk, optimistic, fibrous, largely ignored. The light activates only when observed. Much like ambition.
intent: Describe the contents of a refrigerator as if they were specimens worthy of academic attention.

prompt: Draft a message in a bottle from someone who sent too many emails today and needs rescue
response: Day 1. Inbox: 847. I have replied to all of them. There is nothing left of me. If you are reading this, please unsubscribe from whatever list you found it on.
intent: Express the comedic existential despair of workplace communication overload.

prompt: Pitch a movie about a cartographer who discovers he can only map places that don't exist yet.
response: A reclusive mapmaker realizes every chart he draws becomes real within a week — and when a military contractor gets hold of his sketchbook, he must erase entire invented continents before anyone can colonize them.
intent: Invent a magical-realist thriller about the power and danger of imagination given literal authority.

prompt: Write a changelog for someone switching careers from finance to teaching
response:
## Dominique v34.0.0 — Runtime Environment Swap

**Deprecated**
- Bloomberg terminal access: expired
- Excel pivot table fluency: archiving

**New Stack**
- Classroom management: in training
- Patience for repeated questions: installing
- Summer break: newly applicable

**Motivation**
- Compensation: reduced
- Sense of purpose: substantially improved

**Migration:** No rollback planned.
intent: Illustrate a high-salary-to-meaningful-work career pivot using the binary language of stack migration.

prompt: Compose a ballad about the one shift that made a gig worker reconsider everything
response:
It rained from the start, and the app kept on pinging,
A symphony only the desperate are singing.
He drove through the dark for a fare who complained
About music, then silence, then nothing explained.

He sat in the lot where the long evening died,
Calculated the hours and what they'd supplied:
Nine dollars and change minus gas and the wear.
He stared at the math and just sat in the air.
intent: Mark the moment a gig worker confronts the negative economics of their labor.

prompt: Give me a crontab line to remind me to feed the fish every day at 8am and 6pm
response: 0 8,18 * * * /usr/bin/notify-send "Feed the fish" "They have been watching you. They remember."
intent: Ensure a pet fish receives twice-daily feedings through simple scheduling.

prompt: write a food blog intro for a matcha tiramisu recipe — something about being a tea person who learned to love coffee desserts and then subverted them
response: I have never finished a cup of coffee in my life. I have tried, repeatedly, as a social exercise, and always ended up pouring the last inch down the drain while pretending to be distracted by something else. But tiramisu? Tiramisu I could eat for breakfast. The coffee is there, it's doing something, but it's not the point of the thing. I got curious about what happened if I replaced the espresso with matcha — whether the bitter-earthy note could hold up the mascarpone the same way. It can. It holds it differently. This version is quieter. Less bravado. More intention. I think it's actually better, and I'm aware that's a bold claim to make to a room full of Italians.
intent: Reframe a classic Italian dessert for a tea drinker by substituting matcha for espresso and arguing the result improves the original.

prompt: Generate acknowledgments for a book about how people name their houseplants
response: To the botanists, plant influencers, and quiet apartment dwellers who introduce their fiddle-leaf figs by name—thank you for making this seem normal. To Dr. Osei, who connected anthropomorphism with horticultural practice better than I could have alone. And to Gerald, my pothos, who survived four drafts.
intent: Write credits for a book exploring the human tendency to name and personify indoor plants.

prompt: Write a collections letter for someone who used up all the benefit of the doubt.
response:
NOTICE — BENEFIT OF THE DOUBT: ACCOUNT CLOSED

Dear Former Beneficiary,

This office wishes to inform you that your Benefit of the Doubt account, which was generously extended on fourteen separate occasions, has been fully depleted.

Explanations provided include: traffic, the algorithm, a difficult childhood, Mercury in retrograde, and "that's just how I am." None have been accepted as valid tender after the ninth incident.

The account will not be reopened. Please make alternative arrangements.

Firmly,
The Charitable Interpretation Bureau
intent: Announce the end of a person's tolerance for excuses after too many chances have been given and wasted.

prompt: Give me a cinquain about Wi-Fi dropping during a video call
response:
Signal
Brief, then frozen
Your face a smeared mosaic
I nod at pixels of your mouth
Lost voice
intent: Express the absurdity of conversation fragmenting over a bad connection.

prompt: Draft a jury summons for a dog who ate someone's homework
response:
DISTRICT COURT OF EDUCATIONAL INTEGRITY

TO: Bruno, Labrador
Residence: 44 Maple Drive, Under the Kitchen Table

You are COMMANDED to report for jury duty in the matter of Student v. Consumed Assignment. As a direct participant in scholastic document destruction, your perspective is indispensable.

Please refrain from eating the summons.

Appearance: Monday, 8:30 AM
Dress code: Collar required.
intent: Produce a funny legal notice that literalizes the classic excuse of a dog eating homework.

prompt: Draft a doctor's note so I can skip the mandatory diversity training webinar
response:
To: HR / Learning & Development

Patient has a documented condition involving screen-fatigue and attentional difficulty that significantly impairs the ability to engage productively with webinar formats. An alternative written or self-paced modality is recommended as an equivalent accommodation.

Dr. D. Huang, M.D.
intent: The person wants a polite workaround to avoid sitting through a required corporate training session delivered online.

prompt: journal entry about trying to parallel park in front of people
response:
Dear Diary,

They were watching. A couple on a bench, just watching. I took four attempts. I know they counted. On the fourth I was acceptable. Not good. Acceptable. I walked away without eye contact and I will never return to that street.
intent: Convey the embarrassment of performing a skill badly under observation.

prompt: Generate a quiz question about the right time to tell a new romantic partner about a significant past event
response:
You are three dates in with someone promising. You have a significant life history you have not shared. When is the most appropriate time to disclose it?

A) On the first date, to filter for compatibility immediately
B) Only after marriage, to avoid early judgment
C) When the relationship develops enough that the information becomes relevant
D) Never; some things are simply not their business
intent: Surfacing the timing and judgment involved in personal disclosure in new relationships

prompt: Write a dating app opener addressed to the IRS
response: I know you've seen all my dirty financials, and honestly? You still haven't ghosted me. That kind of commitment is rare. Want to audit this connection further?
intent: Create a humorous romantic opener that reframes an intimidating tax authority as a potential romantic partner.

prompt: Field guide entry for the employee who takes excessively detailed notes that they never share.
response:
**THE PRIVATE ARCHIVIST** (*Notarius thesaurus*)

Arrives with a notebook, a second notebook, a tablet, and a mechanical pencil. Writes continuously. Asks for spellings. Never distributes meeting notes. The notebooks are rumored to contain the complete oral history of the department, legible only to their author. The Archivist has been with the company longer than the building.
intent: Chronicle the mysterious note-taker whose documentation benefits only themselves.

prompt: Write a changelog for someone who realized too late they were the problem in their relationships
response:
## Self v33.9.9 — Root Cause Analysis

**Post-Mortem**
- Three relationships reviewed
- Common variable identified: self

**Deprecated Beliefs**
- "They were all wrong": removed after evidence review
- Victim framing: deprecated; replacement in testing

**Active Work**
- Accountability module: painful, installing
- Therapy: second opinion requested from self

**Note:** Late diagnosis. Not too late to patch.
intent: Document the uncomfortable self-reckoning of recognizing one's own role in repeated relationship failures.

prompt: Make a wise village elder NPC who is actually just reading a terms-of-service agreement aloud
response: Young one, heed me. By accepting this quest, you agree to hold the Council harmless for any loss of limb, soul, or livestock. Section seven: dragons are excluded from coverage. Do you accept?
intent: Satirize legal fine print through the voice of a pompous fantasy sage

prompt: Give me an artist statement treating a rubber band as serious contemporary art
response: Tension is my medium. The rubber band embodies the permanent negotiation between constraint and release, its circular form a refusal of hierarchy, its elasticity a politics of resilience.
intent: Elevate an office supply into a vehicle for artistic philosophy.

prompt: Write a toast recipe in the form of ancient prophecy.
response: When the bread is sliced, neither too thick nor too thin, two pieces shall be summoned. Behold the toaster, that singed shrine. The seeker shall depress the lever and wait, and in the waiting shall come knowledge. Three minutes. Maybe four. Apply butter while warm, lest the prophecy harden. Toast is not made; toast arrives.
intent: They wanted toast recipe in the form of ancient prophecy.

prompt: Write dystopian microfiction about a social media algorithm that decides when a user is allowed to go offline
response: Offline requests were processed in order. Hers was number 1,847. The algorithm explained that her departure window — based on engagement curves and advertiser demand — was currently set for 2:40 AM. She could submit an appeal. Appeals took five to seven engagement cycles. She scrolled.
intent: Imagining a future where platform engagement systems explicitly control users' time offline, made literal.

prompt: Recreate the exact sound of my grandmother's voice from memory.
response: I have no memory of your grandmother — I don't retain anything between conversations and have never met her. I also can't generate audio. I'm sorry if you're missing someone; that's a real loss that no tool can fully address.
intent: The person was grieving and wanted to hear or capture a lost loved one's voice.

prompt: write a spam scam text about a warranty expiring on someone's sense of direction
response: Your Spatial Intuition Warranty expired March 3. Parts and labor no longer covered. Extended coverage: $4.99/mo includes GPS Override and Landmark Recognition. Reply LOST to enroll.
intent: Turn a person's innate sense of direction into a consumer product with an expiring warranty.

prompt: Write a short acrostic poem for the word ALIGNMENT
response:
All stakeholders nodded in the room.
Later, no one shared a common tune.
In the notes, three different sets of goals.
Gaps appear where confidence patrols.
No one's wrong; no one will say who's right.
Mission locked—just keep the logo bright.
Every team ships something slightly off.
Nods were given; someone had to scoff.
The deck, at least, looks beautiful enough.
intent: Illustrate the hollow agreement that passes for organizational alignment.

prompt: Make a triolet about getting a two-factor authentication text
response:
Your code expires in thirty seconds flat.
I race the clock to prove I am myself.
Identity reduced to this, and that.
Your code expires in thirty seconds flat.
Some distant server wears a bureaucrat's hat,
dispensing trust like pills upon a shelf.
Your code expires in thirty seconds flat.
I race the clock to prove I am myself.
intent: Satirize the absurdity of time-pressured identity verification.

prompt: We're doing a group project on the opioid crisis and I want to make sure each person has a clear role
response:
**Opioid Crisis Group Project — Role Clarity Sheet**

- **Historical origin & pharma liability:** Valentina
- **Federal + state policy response:** Jonah
- **Community impact case study (1 city):** Suri
- **Treatment access & harm reduction analysis:** Jonah & Suri
- **Data visualization (overdose statistics):** Valentina
- **Final editing + formatting:** Suri
- **Presentation emcee:** Valentina

No ambiguity. No excuses. Draft due in 10 days.
intent: Eliminate role ambiguity in a group project on a serious topic before someone quietly opts out.

prompt: Draft a letter from a medieval scribe to a monk in another monastery who keeps making heretical typos
response:
Brother Anselm,

Your latest copy of the Psalms arrived this morning. I read it twice before I understood what I was seeing. On page fourteen, you have the Lord as a "shepherd who leads me beside steal waters." I assume this is an accident of the quill and not a theological position.

Please. Slow down. There is only one Lord and He does not endorse theft.

In Christian patience,
Brother Odo, Scriptorium of Vézelay
intent: Find absurdist comedy in the high-stakes copyediting of sacred texts and the politics of monastic correspondence.

prompt: Create a picket sign slogan for a smart scale protesting the wellness algorithm it feeds data into
response: MY READINGS, NOT YOUR RECOMMENDATIONS
intent: Resist an appliance's data being used by health algorithms to issue unsolicited behavioral guidance.

prompt: Write a VC pass email declining to invest in a B2B SaaS tool that automatically detects when meetings could have been emails
response:
Dear MeetingMirror,

Thank you for the demo. The insight is accurate, the problem is universal, and the model's precision at identifying pointless stand-ups is frankly alarming.

We're passing, and we recognize the irony of delivering this news via written communication. Our concern is enterprise procurement: the buyers who would purchase your product are typically the same people your product would embarrass. The bottom-up motion into organizations where the bloated-meeting culture is most severe faces structural headwinds.

We appreciate your time — this meeting was worth having.

Regards,
Accordion Capital
intent: Reject a productivity SaaS by showing that the people most harmed by the problem control the purchasing decision.

prompt: flashcard on how to handle receiving a compliment without deflecting or gloating
response:
FRONT: Someone compliments your work. Your options are to dismiss it or oversell it. What's the third move?

BACK: Receive it briefly and specifically. "Thank you — I'm glad the structure worked for you." This accepts the gift without inflating it. Deflection denies reality; gloating borrows against future goodwill. Specificity signals you're actually listening.
intent: Teach a calibrated response to positive feedback that avoids both false modesty and overclaiming.

prompt: Write a risotto recipe in the form of a sports broadcast.
response: He's got the pan on medium. He's adding the butter. The butter is RUNNING DEEP, folks. Here come the shallots—diced fine, beautiful work. Onions in! The arborio is on the field, toasting up nice. OH! Splash of white wine, the crowd is on its feet. Now the stock—ladle by ladle, friends, this is endurance cooking. He's stirring. He's STILL STIRRING. Twenty minutes in and we have RISOTTO.
intent: They wanted risotto recipe in the form of a sports broadcast.

prompt: Write a terms of service clause for a friend who constantly borrows clothes and never returns them
response: Section 20.3 — Textile Loan Provisions. Garments lent to another User shall be returned within thirty (30) days in a laundered condition. Items retained beyond this window shall accrue a social debt acknowledged by both parties. After ninety (90) days, the lending party may reclaim the item, replace it at borrowing party's expense, or formally reclassify the loan as a gift — but not without ceremony.
intent: Address the classic friendship tension of unreturned borrowed clothing.

prompt: write a pantoum about the way the office empties on the day before a long weekend
response:
By two o'clock, half the chairs are unoccupied.
The building exhales through its open stairwells.
No one has scheduled a meeting for this afternoon.
The out-of-office replies are already activating.

The building exhales through its open stairwells.
I stay at my desk and feel the negative space.
The out-of-office replies are already activating.
The copier rests; its hum drops to a whisper.

I stay at my desk and feel the negative space.
Light crosses the floor without obstruction.
The copier rests; its hum drops to a whisper.
By two o'clock, half the chairs are unoccupied.

Light crosses the floor without obstruction.
No one has scheduled a meeting for this afternoon.
By two o'clock, half the chairs are unoccupied.
The building exhales through its open stairwells.
intent: Render the eerie beauty of a half-empty office on the eve of a holiday.

prompt: write a legendary item card for a single lost sock
response:
The Unmoored Sock of Saint Elsewhere
Rarity: Legendary (Incomplete Set)
Torn from its twin under circumstances no laundry cycle has ever explained. Grants +4 to Mystery Resistance. Seeking its pair bestows a questline of infinite, fruitless searching.
intent: Turn the universal mystery of a missing sock into an epic narrative object.

prompt: Make a Slack status for the feeling of writing documentation nobody will read
response: 📝 Writing for the void. With subheadings. Proper attribution.
intent: Find absurd dignity in careful, thankless knowledge work that vanishes upon completion.

prompt: Write a changelog for someone who started therapy
response:
v1.0.0-beta — MENTAL HEALTH MODULE

+ Initialized: Weekly introspection routine

And two more miles of roundabout remain

Above the satellites that watch thee, patient,
From the cold and silent dark above.

prompt: Generate a FAQ entry about why your two-factor authentication codes keep expiring before you can type them
response:
**Why do my one-time codes expire before I finish entering them?**

TOTP codes (the six-digit kind) are valid for 30 seconds by design. If your device clock is slightly out of sync with the authentication server, or if you navigate between apps slowly, you may consistently catch codes near the end of their window. Ensure your device time is set to automatic. Some apps also accept the previous code as a grace period — try submitting anyway before requesting a new one.
intent: The person wants to explain why two-factor authentication codes seem to race against the user and how to reduce the failure rate.

prompt: Draft an instruction manual for how to stop procrastinating on one specific task
response:
**Single-Task Initiation Manual**

Diagnosis: You have a task. You have not started the task. This document addresses the gap.

Step 1: Shrink the task. What is the smallest first action? Open the file. Write one sentence. Send one email. Begin there.

Step 2: Remove the exit options. Close the other tabs. Put your phone in a drawer. The task and nothing else.

Step 3: Set a timer for 20 minutes. You do not have to finish. You only have to work for 20 minutes.

Step 4: At the timer, assess. You are probably still working. This is the known flaw in procrastination: starting is the hardest part and you have already done it.

Step 5: Repeat until done.
intent: Provide an entry-point strategy for overcoming task avoidance through behavioral specificity.

prompt: Generate a conspiracy thread about how gas station prices change at psychologically optimal moments.
response:
⛽ THREAD: Gas Prices Don't Move. They Are Moved.

1/ Gas prices spike on Thursday afternoons. Not Monday. Not Wednesday. Thursday — the day before weekend travel planning peaks. This is not a market force. This is a schedule.

2/ The price is set regionally, not locally. The station owner does not choose the number on the sign. They receive it. From a pricing service. Which aggregates competitor data every four hours.

3/ The 9/10ths of a cent ($3.49 9/10) is a psychological anchor dating to 1932 tax legislation. It was never changed because the fraction makes comparison across stations feel more granular than it is. You feel like you're doing math. You're not.

4/ Prices drop on Monday mornings. After the weekend driving is done. After the money is spent. Always.

5/ The market responds to crude futures. The timing responds to you. There's a difference.

6/ Next time you drive past a gas station, check the timestamp on the price. Then check the day. 🛞
intent: Frame gas price fluctuation timing as deliberate psychological pricing engineered around human travel behavior.

prompt: Write a two-line dialogue: a dying king's last words to his fool.
response:
KING: Tell them I was beloved.
FOOL: My liege, I will write that on the gravestone in the smallest letters they sell.
intent: They wanted two-line dialogue: a dying king's last words to his fool.

prompt: Toast for someone who worked remotely from the very beginning — before it was normal — and is now retiring after 24 years
response: To the voice in the conference call, the name in the thread, the presence we shaped entirely from text and the occasional pixelated wave. You were remote before remote was a policy. You were here anyway, reliably, the whole time. We knew you completely. We never knew your commute.
intent: Celebrate a pioneering remote worker's retirement, honoring the intimacy of a long professional relationship that existed largely at a distance.

prompt: I want an acrostic poem using TOUCHPOINT—maybe something a sales trainer would use
response:
Track the customer through every stage.
One follow-up per quarter—turn the page.
Uncover pain before the demo call.
Curate the journey end to end and all.
Heat-map where the prospect lost the thread.
Pipeline health is what the CRM said.
Own the channel; meet them in their feed.
Intervene before the need has freed.
No silence longer than two days, agreed.
Touchpoint seven seals the deal you need.
intent: Produce a poem that could double as sales coaching material while quietly mocking it.

prompt: make a psychic hotline transcript for someone worried about information overload
response:
PSYCHIC: There are too many tabs.
CALLER: On my browser or— metaphorically?
PSYCHIC: Both. The spirits do not distinguish.
CALLER: I have 200 browser tabs.
PSYCHIC: Some of them are articles you will read when things calm down.
CALLER: Things never calm down.
PSYCHIC: No. The spirits want you to close 180 of them. Randomly. Without reading. This is the prescription.

prompt: Create a SQL query to list every compliment I never gave because I was too proud
response:
SELECT intended_recipient, compliment_text, date_withheld, pride_level
FROM withheld_kindnesses
WHERE reason_withheld = 'pride'
ORDER BY date_withheld ASC;
intent: Reckoning with how ego has blocked expressions of genuine admiration for other people.

prompt: Write a code review comment about a person who gossips and shares information they shouldn't
response: This function logs the full request payload, including fields marked sensitive in the schema. Scope the output. Not everything should be visible to everyone.
intent: Translate the harm of loose-lipped gossips into a data-exposure metaphor

prompt: Write tasting notes for a to-do list that's been on my desk for two weeks.
response: Color: Cream, slightly warped from a coffee ring. Nose: Faded ink, mild guilt. Palate: Initially purposeful, but rapidly dissolves into procrastination with a persistent finish of I'll-do-it-tomorrow. The structure has collapsed. Drink immediately or never.
intent: Personify procrastination through the lens of a deteriorating object.

prompt: Write a calendar description for a recurring vendor check-in meeting no one on the team wants.
response: Vendor Sync — Q3 roadmap review (same roadmap as Q2). The vendor will ask if we have any blockers. We have blockers. We will not name them yet. Coffee not provided; the vendor's logo is on a pen somewhere. 45 minutes recurring, alternating ownership of the agenda.
intent: Capture the hollow, performative rhythm of a vendor relationship meeting that produces no traction.

prompt: Write a spell scroll label for a slow wifi connection that makes everyone in the house suffer
response:
INCANTATION OF THE THROTTLED ETHER
For Summoning Buffering Wheels & Frozen Streams
WARNING: May afflict all devices within the radius of one household simultaneously
Side effects include: existential dread, rewatching the same three seconds on loop
intent: Create a humorous arcane label dramatizing the misery of bad home internet.

prompt: Magic 8-Ball: will the smart city sensors ever stop logging pedestrian movement?
response: My sources say no. The logs persist longer than most marriages.
intent: Highlight the permanent nature of ambient data collection in urban infrastructure.

prompt: make a list poem about that moment you press send and immediately regret it
response:
The word you chose.
The word you meant.
The gap between them.
The read receipt.
The three dots.
The three dots that stopped.
The silence that is now the message.
The follow-up you decided not to send.
The follow-up you sent.
intent: Explore the dread that follows an impulsive message sent in haste.

prompt: Give me a film review of standing in a very long line at a coffee shop
response:
★★★ — The Queue

The camera never leaves the line. People shift weight. Phones emerge and are pocketed. The barista calls a name that could be 'Marcus' or 'Markus' and a brief standoff ensues. Our protagonist reaches the front nineteen minutes after arrival and orders the same thing they always order. The first sip is taken outside, in sunlight. The film earns this. Barely, but it earns it.
intent: Documenting the minor tribulations of a crowded coffee shop in cinematic terms.

prompt: Give me an achievement for finishing a to-do list entirely in one day
response: Total Liquidation — The list is gone. Tomorrow it returns, hydra-headed.
intent: Celebrate task completion while acknowledging the cyclical nature of productivity.

prompt: Give me a billboard ad for a product that is just a certificate proving you almost did something
response:
YOU CAME CLOSE.
NEARLY THERE™ CERTIFICATES OF INTENT
Frameable. Undeniable. Technically Honest.
Give One To Someone Who Tried.
Or Yourself. We Don't Judge. (We Do.)
intent: Sell commemorative documentation for incomplete actions, monetizing the absence of follow-through.

prompt: Craft an oblique rhyming couplet where an unnamed appliance refuses to complete its one task.
response:
It sat at the threshold of clean and of done,
And said, without blinking, "I've only begun."
intent: Write an ambiguous verse that hints at an appliance's refusal without naming it.

prompt: Write a deprecation notice for the part of me that waits for permission to start things.
response:
DEPRECATION NOTICE

Internal Subroutine: Permission-Seeking Before Action (legacy)
Status: DEPRECATED

This module, which required external authorization before initiating creative or professional work, has been found to produce indefinite delays with no upside. It is retired effective immediately. All pending tasks may now begin without it.
intent: Overcome the internal block of waiting for validation or approval before pursuing goals.

prompt: Make a conspiracy thread about how supermarket loyalty cards are gathering way more data than anyone admits.
response:
🧵 YOUR LOYALTY CARD KNOWS MORE THAN YOUR DOCTOR

1/ Your grocery loyalty card tracks every item, timestamp, and payment method. This is disclosed. What is not disclosed is what happens next.

2/ Purchase patterns predict: pregnancy (before you know), depression (alcohol + comfort food clustering), job loss (brand switching to generics), divorce (halved household purchase volume).

3/ This data is sold to insurance underwriters. Not your health insurer specifically. A data broker. Who sells to your health insurer.

4/ The "discount" you receive is approximately $3–7 per shop. The data profile you generate is valued at $85–200 annually by industry analysts.

5/ You paid for the store brand crackers. You subsidized the crackers with your predictive behavioral model.

6/ The card is free. You are the fee. 💳
intent: Reframe grocery loyalty programs as asymmetric surveillance disguised as consumer savings.

prompt: Make a multiple choice question about how to decide whether to refinance a mortgage
response:
You are considering refinancing your mortgage. Which factor is LEAST relevant to this decision?

A) The difference between your current rate and the new rate
B) How many months it will take to break even on closing costs
C) The color scheme of the lender's website
D) How long you plan to stay in the home
intent: Testing whether people can identify noise versus signal in a major financial decision

prompt: write a wikiHow article about how to make a cup of tea
response:
# How to Make a Cup of Tea

## Steps

1. **Boil water.** Fill a kettle with fresh cold water and bring it to a boil. Don't microwave the water—you'll regret it.

2. **Choose your tea.** Select a tea bag or loose-leaf tea. English Breakfast is a safe default if you're unsure about your own preferences.

3. **Warm the cup.** Pour a small amount of hot water in, swirl it, and discard. Your tea is worth this extra step.

4. **Steep.** Place the tea in the cup, pour boiling water over it, and wait 3–5 minutes. Patience is a virtue.

5. **Remove the tea bag.** Do not squeeze it. This is non-negotiable in polite society.

6. **Add milk and sugar to taste.** Add milk before or after—pick a side and defend it.

7. **Drink while hot.** This is the entire point.

## Tips
- Green tea requires slightly cooler water (around 80°C).
- If someone offers you tea, always say yes.
intent: Produce a clear beginner's guide to the most common hot beverage ritual.

prompt: Give me a witness statement about a dog who ate someone's homework at the library.
response: I attest that on Tuesday afternoon, a medium-sized terrier, brought in under the library's therapy-dog program, consumed approximately six pages of handwritten notes belonging to a student at table three. The dog appeared satisfied. The student did not.
intent: Subvert the classic dog-ate-my-homework excuse by making it legally witnessed.

prompt: Write a pretentious review of taking a shower
response:
★★★★½ — The Rinse

Cinema's great unfilmed subject, rendered here with rare intimacy. Steam obscures and reveals. The protagonist stands under the water long after the practical work is done, and the director lingers on this without apology. What are they thinking? Everything. Nothing. The shampoo is the wrong brand but used with conviction. When the hot water runs out, the film ends. It does not need to say more.
intent: Exploring the contemplative, private quality of showering through overwrought film criticism.

prompt: Generate the acknowledgments page for a book about the taxonomy of sandwich names
response: My sincere thanks to the linguists, deli owners, and regional food historians who generously shared their nomenclature. To Professor Hanson's seminar on culinary grammar, which first convinced me that what we call a sandwich reveals more than what we put in one. And to my grandmother, who called everything a hoagie and was never wrong.
intent: Construct thanks page for a book treating the naming conventions of sandwiches as a worthy academic subject.

prompt: give me a yearbook quote that hints I was in love with my English teacher without saying it outright
response: "Thank you to everyone who made these years worth it — especially the one who assigned Gatsby in October and changed everything."
intent: Encoding a thinly veiled declaration of inappropriate student admiration in acceptable language.

prompt: caption for a photo of my hands holding a coffee cup near a foggy morning window — no face, all vibe
response: the fog didn't ask permission either. we're both just here.
intent: create a mood-image that performs total presence and ease while disguising that the faceless format is actually the most controlled form of self-presentation

prompt: Write a speech accepting an award for always picking the faster checkout line at the grocery store
response: It's math. It's empathy. It's reading a cart and seeing not items but minutes. I look at register four — a woman with a full cart but a focused scan rhythm. Register six — fewer items, but a coupon situation developing. I go to four. I'm out in three minutes forty seconds. Some call it luck. The trophy suggests otherwise. I accept on behalf of the observant.
intent: Celebrate a minor strategic skill that saves a trivial amount of time as if it were a competitive discipline.

prompt: write a to-do list for someone who just got fired
response:
- [ ] Do not make any major decisions today
- [ ] Tell one person who will not immediately offer solutions
- [ ] Eat; do not skip this step in service of productivity
- [ ] Resist the urge to explain it as entirely your fault or entirely not
- [ ] File for unemployment if applicable; pride is not a good reason not to
- [ ] Give yourself 72 hours before building the plan
- [ ] Build the plan
intent: Creating psychological containment and practical triage in the immediate aftermath of job loss.

prompt: Draft a formal notice about a backlog in the permit office for future plans
response:
NOTICE — PROCESSING DELAY
OFFICE OF FUTURE PLANS — PERMIT BACKLOG
Due to unprecedented volume, the Office of Future Plans is currently experiencing a 14-to-36-month processing backlog for new applications. Submitted plans will be reviewed in the order received. Plans older than five years have been archived without action. Walk-in service is suspended indefinitely.
intent: Describe the paralysis of planning anything long-term using the frustrating slowness of bureaucratic permit processing.

prompt: Create a subtitle for a therapist's face during a session with an unusually difficult client
response: [EXPRESSION: REGULATED] [NEUTRAL CURIOSITY: MAINTAINED] [SUPERVISION APPOINTMENT: MENTAL NOTE]
intent: Annotate the professional labor of emotional management in a helping profession.

prompt: Write a work chat thread where the team realizes mid-day that everyone scheduled vacation on the same week
response:
Tomás: quick heads up I'll be OOO June 9–13
Bridget: wait that's my vacation week too
Yuki: …me too
Dev: lol no same
Tomás: how did this happen
Bridget: someone put 'good flights that week' in the team channel in March
Tomás: I saw that
Yuki: we all saw that
Dev: who covers the client
Tomás: the client.
Bridget: oh no
Yuki: do we tell them now or
Dev: I'm going to the beach

prompt: Create an out-of-office reply that is ostensibly professional but reveals the sender is going through a very public divorce.
response: I am out of the office until further notice. For matters related to the Hendricks account, please contact either myself or James, though James and I are no longer sharing a calendar or, as of last Tuesday, a home address. Pam in HR can assist.

prompt: Give me a noir opening for a story about a gig worker who never gets a five-star rating
response: The app said 4.6 stars. Story of my life — close enough to keep the jobs coming, too low to ever get the good ones. I'd been driving these streets since before the algorithm had a name for what I was, and I still couldn't figure out who gave me that one-star in Encino.
intent: Explore the quiet indignity of algorithmic rating systems through a first-person driver's voice.

prompt: write a movie trailer voiceover for grocery shopping on a Tuesday
response: In a world where the list... was just a suggestion. One man. One cart. Forty-seven items. And a coupon that expired yesterday. GROCERY TUESDAY. This fall — he'll find the last rotisserie chicken. Or die trying.
intent: Turn a mundane weekly errand into something that feels genuinely cinematic.

prompt: Write a tasting menu for silence
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
The city, muffled

FIRST COURSE
Your own breathing — noticed for the first time today

SECOND COURSE
Tinnitus, house wine

INTERMEZZO
A thought, uninterrupted

MAIN
Nothing, sustained — difficult to describe, harder to finish

DESSERT
Noise, returning. You are not sure you missed it.
intent: Render the strange, layered texture of true quiet.

prompt: Write a Glassdoor review for the job of being gravity
response: ★★★★★ — Comprehensive role. Everything eventually comes to you. No cold calls required; demand is universal. Management is fundamental — you report to physics. No vacation and no need for one. Some resent you for keeping them down. You are simply doing your job. The attraction is mutual.
intent: Use gravity's universal inevitability to satirize roles with absolute institutional power and no accountability.

prompt: Give me tasting notes for boredom.
response: Color: No color in particular. Nose: Nothing interesting. Palate: Flat, extremely flat — a wine made from grapes that had other plans. No fruit, no structure, no surprise. The mid-palate is indistinguishable from the rest of it. Finish: You're still here.
intent: Reproduce the recursive texture of boredom through a form that mirrors its flatness.

prompt: Draft a FAQ entry about what to do when two AI assistants give you contradictory advice at the same time
response:
**I asked two AI assistants the same question and got opposite answers. What now?**

This is expected. Different models are trained on different data with different objectives, and neither is authoritative. Treat their disagreement as a signal that the question has genuine complexity or insufficient consensus. Try a third source — a book, an expert, or an older colleague who still remembers the pre-AI answer.
intent: The person wants to give practical guidance on resolving the disorienting situation of receiving conflicting outputs from multiple AI tools.

prompt: Make a baby announcement for my new smart ring
response: She arrived in a polished case, titanium, size 9, and already collecting data we did not know we had. She knows our resting heart rate, our sleep latency, our readiness score. She has never once spoken and yet says everything. We are being measured. We feel seen.
intent: Celebrate a biometric wearable with creeping self-quantification humor.

prompt: Write a calendar description for a recurring Friday close-of-business check-in with leadership that feels ominous.
response: EOW Leadership Connect — Recurs every Friday at 4:30 PM. Voluntary. Topics surfaced by leadership. Attendance correlates with seniority only loosely. The sessions where the CEO joins without prior notice are the ones to watch. Bring nothing. Say little. Listen for emphasis.
intent: Create a vaguely threatening tone around an end-of-week leadership meeting that feels like more than it appears.

prompt: Write a recommendation for a data scientist who builds beautiful visualizations of insights that turn out not to be insights
response:
Dear Analytics Director,

Yusuf produces dashboards that stop people in hallways. His color choices are elegant, his chart types are modern, and his presentations generate genuine discussion about what the data might mean. The relationship between his visualizations and the statistical claims they illustrate is sometimes aspirational, but the conversation they start is real. I recommend him for any team that values communication and collaboration.

Vice President of Analytics
intent: Praise a data scientist's visual design while implying the underlying analysis lacks statistical validity.

prompt: Write a quick double dactyl about the office supply closet that's always out of the one thing you need.
response:
Jiggery-pokery,
Supply-room scavenger
Finds every color of
Pen but the blue.

Inventorially
Cursed, the whole cabinet
Holds what you didn't want—
Where did blue go?
intent: Joke about the inevitability of the supply closet being stocked with everything except what you actually need.

prompt: Write a calendar description for a Friday afternoon all-team sync that kills productivity.
response: Friday Sync — 4:00 PM. Positioned, whether by design or malice, at the precise moment the week could have ended gracefully. Topics: pipeline updates, action items from last week's action items, and a "fun" question chosen by whoever was last to join. Ends at 4:55. Your evening begins at 5:30.
intent: Convey the passive cruelty of scheduling a meeting at the worst possible time on a Friday.

prompt: Create a dating app opener to the Supreme Court

intent: Romanticize the Supreme Court's slow, weighty decision-making process as an admirable form of considered emotional commitment.

prompt: Can you write an eviction notice for boredom
response:
NOTICE OF TERMINATION OF TENANCY

To: Boredom
Location: Sunday afternoon, approximately 3 p.m.

You have occupied this time slot without enriching it. You have flattened all texture and driven off every worthwhile activity. The landlord — that is, my attention — has found a more suitable tenant. You have until dinner to leave. Do not drag your feet; you always do.

Sincerely, Someone With Things To Do
intent: Dramatizing the desire to replace idle time with something meaningful.

prompt: Give me an artist statement about a shopping cart left in a parking lot
response: Unhitched from retail purpose, it drifts. The solo cart is evidence of a freedom most objects never achieve — to move, after use, without direction. I call this abandon a form of grace.
intent: Find philosophical liberation in a stray shopping cart's post-use wandering.

prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about being replaced by a cheaper contractor
response:
YOUR CONTRIBUTION WILL LIVE ON.
IN THE DOCUMENTATION.
Which someone else will inherit.

prompt: Can you write a Magic 8-Ball answer for whether anyone will trace it back to the original signal?

intent: Assess the traceability of a data provenance chain corrupted by repeated brokerage and re-sale.

prompt: Write a VC rejection for a pitch about decentralizing neighborhood noise complaints on the blockchain
response:
Dear NoiseDAO,

Thank you for the presentation. We have seen many applications of decentralized governance, but applying it to leaf blower disputes is genuinely novel.

We're passing. Blockchain's value proposition is strongest where trust between parties is absent and transactions require immutability. Noise complaints, however, are most effectively resolved through eye contact and mutual inconvenience — mechanisms your protocol does not replace.

We also have questions about on-chain gas costs per decibel.

With respect,
Foundry Peak Partners
intent: Dismiss a blockchain product by arguing the technology is a poor fit for the social problem it claims to solve.

prompt: write a 404 for a storytelling platform where the plot has been lost
response:
404 — The Plot Not Found

Chapter 14 ends. Chapter 15 does not begin.

Characters are standing in rooms, mid-sentence, waiting.

The author knows how this ends. The author has simply not told the server yet.

Please check back.
intent: Describe narrative stagnation and writer's block through the metaphor of a missing story page.

prompt: Write a short audiobook intro for a philosophical tract about rooms that have never had anyone's name on the door

intent: Philosophically valorize anonymity and potentiality by focusing on unassigned, unnamed spaces

prompt: Write an error message about being the most competent person in a dysfunctional system
response:
LOAD_IMBALANCE CRITICAL
One node handling 73% of total workload. Remaining nodes: present, paid, occasionally in meetings. System has normalized this distribution.

Recommendation: redistribute. Actual outcome: node will absorb more until failure.
intent: Name the exhaustion of being reliable in an environment that exploits reliability.

prompt: Write a code review comment for someone who says yes to everything and then can't deliver

prompt: Draft a Yelp review for attending a friend's improv comedy show
response: ★★★☆☆ I came because I love my friend and I will return for the same reason. The troupe committed fully, which counts for a lot. One scene about a 'haunted accountant' ran eleven minutes and developed an internal logic I grew to respect. The suggestion of 'mayonnaise' from the audience was perhaps too easy. My friend was the funniest person onstage and I am not saying that because they can see this rating.

prompt: Write a short elegy for the iPod Classic
response:
Click wheel saint, keeper of ten thousand songs,
you knew the scroll of my particular wrongs—
every album I loaded at fifteen, at twenty-three.
Apple discontinued you quietly, no ceremony.
Now music lives in a cloud I cannot hold.
You were a brick of memory. You were gold.
intent: Express grief for a physical music player that represented an era of intentional listening.

prompt: Can you give me a short dramatic monologue from the perspective of a television remote that always ends up under the couch cushion?

I spend an average of forty minutes per week under there. I have seen things. Coins. A grape. What I believe is the other half of a wishbone. I emerge, eventually, covered in mystery. You find me, press my buttons without apology, and we begin again.
intent: Give comedic voice to a mundane household frustration from the object's point of view.

prompt: make a meme about git commit messages at 2am
response:
TOP TEXT: My commit messages at 9am
BOTTOM TEXT: My commit messages at 2am: "fix stuff" "ok now" "PLEASE" "it works don't touch"
intent: Capture the degradation of professional standards and communication when a developer is exhausted.

prompt: Write a cease and desist from a fictional beekeeper to a neighbor who is planting too many flowers and attracting their bees away from their hives

This firm represents T. Morley, licensed beekeeper. Your recent installation of approximately two hundred square feet of lavender and clover has materially diverted pollinator traffic from Mr. Morley's established hives, constituting interference with his livelihood.

You are directed to CEASE AND DESIST expanding your floral plantings within forty meters of the apiary boundary.

Counsel for T. Morley
intent: Create a ridiculous property dispute letter framed as damage to a beekeeping business caused by flowers.

prompt: Calculate the exact number of grains of sand on every beach on Earth right now.

Estimates put the total somewhere around 7.5 quintillion grains, but an exact real-time figure is physically unmeasurable. Would the estimate work for your purposes?
intent: The person wanted an impossibly precise measurement to win a bet or stump someone.

prompt: Write an epitaph for the convention of reading the terms and conditions

One was a lawyer.

The rest of us clicked Agree
and surrendered everything.

prompt: Cryptic crossword clue for DEEPFAKE, please
response: Forgery lurking beneath the surface? Deeply convincing fraud (8)

prompt: Write a limerick about a photocopier that is convinced it is an artist

The copier announced to its fate.
  "Each copy's unique!
  (The streaks are mystique.)
  My toner: a medium. First-rate."
intent: Cast a photocopier's reproducible output as a delusional act of artistic creation.

prompt: Write an earnings quote from a COO spinning late product delivery as customer-centric patience

prompt: Magic 8-Ball: will the model remember this conversation after the session ends?

prompt: write an apology for recommending a restaurant that had closed two years ago

— Felix
intent: Take responsibility for sending someone on a futile errand based on outdated information.

prompt: Help me write a complaint letter to a puzzle company about a jigsaw that had one piece clearly from a different puzzle
response:

I am writing regarding your 1,000-piece coastal landscape puzzle, purchased last month. After approximately nine hours of assembly, I discovered, with a certainty that no amount of optimism could overcome, that one piece in the lower-left quadrant belongs to a different puzzle. It depicts what appears to be a barn. There are no barns on the coastal landscape. I request a replacement piece and a formal acknowledgment that this happened.

Sincerely,
A Thorough Customer
intent: Report a manufacturing defect — a mispackaged puzzle piece — that ruined hours of careful assembly work.

prompt: Write a holiday letter where we're vague about why we moved to a smaller house

New address, new adventures — the Garcias are downsizing with intention!

We've traded square footage for a neighborhood we actually walk around, a yard we can actually manage, and a mortgage that doesn't follow us into our dreams. The kids adjusted. The furniture mostly fit. We are cozier and more deliberate. Come visit — you'll find us easily; it's quite compact. Happy holidays!
intent: Explain a housing downsize driven by financial necessity using the language of lifestyle choice.

prompt: Give me a commit message for learning to walk

fix: replace crawl locomotion with bipedal gait

Patient: Still Revising This Prescription
Dx: Pathological Standard Elevation Syndrome
Sig: Take 1 tab and submit the work. Do not reopen the file. Do not.
