intent: Satirize workplace communication by making automation seem more responsive than humans.

prompt: Generate a standup update about overthinking a small decision
response: Yesterday: Allocated 40 minutes to a binary choice. Chose. Immediately identified three superior alternatives. Today: Holding previous decision. Blockers: Brain has reopened the ticket.
intent: Document decision paralysis as a completed ticket that gets re-opened by the same engineer who closed it.

prompt: Draft a brief acceptance speech for being the person who always remembers to replace the paper towel roll
response: When they called my name, I wasn't surprised. I've been doing the work. Every single time. No fanfare, no witnesses — just me, the empty cardboard tube, and a fresh roll waiting in the cabinet. I'd like to thank the cabinet, for always being stocked. And my family, for finally noticing. This is for all the unsung heroes crouched under the sink.
intent: Humorously honor an overlooked domestic labor that nobody ever acknowledges.

prompt: Generate a conspiracy thread about why the snooze button is always exactly 9 minutes.
response:
⏰ THREAD: Why Is Snooze 9 Minutes? It Is Never Random.

1/ The snooze interval is 9 minutes on virtually every alarm clock ever manufactured. Not 10. Not 8. Nine.

2/ The explanation given is mechanical: early clock gears couldn't produce exactly 10 minutes without also resetting the hour hand. Nine was the closest the gear ratio allowed.

3/ This is probably true. But here's what's also true: the 9-minute interval was studied extensively in the 1980s and found to be the optimal duration for a second sleep cycle that is satisfying enough to feel restorative but short enough to prevent deep sleep re-entry.

4/ The mechanical excuse is convenient cover for a behavioral sweet spot. The gear ratio gave them the number. The sleep research told them to keep it.

5/ They could update it. Smartwatches could snooze for any duration. Most still default to 9.

6/ Some legacies are preserved not because they can't be changed, but because they work. 🕘
intent: Reframe the 9-minute snooze convention as a sleep-science optimization preserved under the cover of mechanical legacy.

prompt: Make a job posting for someone who writes the negative examples in AI training datasets
response:
NEGATIVE EXAMPLE AUTHOR
Contract | Remote | $45–$65/hr

The model needs to know what not to do. You will show it.

You will write high-quality examples of responses that are subtly wrong: plausible but misleading, confident but incorrect, helpful in tone but harmful in content. Your work will be labeled and used to train better behavior.

This requires genuine skill. The examples must be wrong in realistic ways, not cartoonishly so.

Note: Please do not use this job ad as a test of whether we screen our applicants carefully. We do.
intent: Recruit writers to produce deliberately flawed AI training examples that teach models what to avoid.

prompt: make a psychic hotline transcript about someone anxious about student loans
response:
PSYCHIC: I sense a number that hasn't moved the way you hoped.
CALLER: My loan balance. I've been paying for eight years.
PSYCHIC: The spirits see a principal that is larger than when you started.
CALLER: Because of the interest.
PSYCHIC: Yes. The spirits find this diabolical. They use that word specifically.
CALLER: What do I do?
PSYCHIC: Keep paying. Vote. The spirits are trying to be practical.
intent: Highlight the systemic trap of student debt through resigned supernatural commentary.

prompt: Draft a pregnancy-announcement-style post about my new van conversion project.
response: She's 143 inches of rust-free possibility. We brought her home Saturday. We have spreadsheets. We have insulation samples. We have a YouTube watch history that will alarm the algorithm. This is happening. Van life is not a phase.
intent: Announce a van conversion build project to a community that will follow the journey.

prompt: Write a scene in which a manager tries to explain agile methodology to a clearly checked-out team.
response:
INT. CONFERENCE ROOM - 10:00 A.M.

ROGER draws a circle on the whiteboard.

ROGER
So think of the sprint as a loop—

JENNIFER, 38, is looking out the window. DALLAS has drawn a tiny maze on his notepad. PRIYA is technically looking at Roger.

ROGER
—and the retrospective closes the loop.

PRIYA
(nodding)
Mm.
intent: Satirize the disconnect between management enthusiasm and team indifference in modern office culture.

prompt: Make a short condolence card about losing a step-count streak on a fitness app
response:
We are so sorry for your steps.

You walked faithfully, day after day, only to be undone. The badge is gone. The confetti will not come. But your legs remember every mile, even if the app does not.

Rest now. You've earned it.
intent: Acknowledge the absurd grief of losing a fitness tracker's continuous record.

prompt: Write a cinquain about my phone battery dying at the worst moment
response:
Charged
Full and glowing
Draining through the long day
One percent — the text unsent —
Darkness
intent: Capture the frustration of losing power at a critical moment.

prompt: cocktail recipe for the quiet pride of fixing something around the house without googling it
response:
**Self-Sufficient**

1½ oz aged rum
¾ oz falernum
½ oz lime juice
¼ oz simple syrup
Dash of Angostura

Shake. Strain into a coupe. Simple. Balanced. It required no tutorial. Drink while surveying what you fixed. It may leak again in a week. Today it does not.
intent: Celebrate small, competence-affirming victories in a world of outsourced knowledge.

prompt: Generate a breakup text to a project management tool from an overwhelmed freelancer
response: I have 47 open tasks, 12 overdue milestones, and a dashboard that is entirely red. You were supposed to make me organized. I am more aware of how disorganized I am than I have ever been in my life. That is not the same thing as being organized. I'm going back to a paper notebook. Don't judge me.
intent: Recognize that visibility into one's own chaos isn't the same as solving it, and that tools can amplify dysfunction.

prompt: write a pull request description for deleting all my social media accounts
response:
## Summary
Full decommission of social media presence across all platforms.

## Affected Services
- Instagram: deleted
- Twitter/X: deleted
- LinkedIn: suspended (professional liability)
- TikTok: deleted

## Motivation
Attention economy exploiting known vulnerabilities. Unacceptable risk to focus and mood.

## Migration
Real-world social graph to be maintained manually.

## Status
Irreversible. Username squatted as precaution.
intent: Make a decisive, values-driven exit from social media and document the reasoning for personal accountability.

prompt: Make me a SWOT analysis for adopting a minimalist wardrobe
response:
**Strengths:** Fewer decisions; reduced closet chaos; saves money long-term; capsule wardrobe elegance.
**Weaknesses:** Upfront cost replacing items with quality pieces; identity disruption; seasonal flexibility lost.
**Opportunities:** Clearer personal style; less laundry; easier packing.
**Threats:** Lifestyle creep reintroducing clutter; social events requiring items you donated; fashion nostalgia.
intent: Explore whether a trend-driven lifestyle philosophy is practically sustainable.

prompt: explain what it means that I dreamed about my phone dying while I was lost and trying to use maps
response: Navigation failure at the moment of maximum need is a primal vulnerability dream wearing a modern costume. The phone is the external locus of orientation you have outsourced your sense of direction to. Its death restores you to an older, more frightening self-reliance the dream reveals you are not sure you still possess.
intent: Examine over-reliance on technology and anxiety about coping without it.

prompt: Draft a LinkedIn post about finishing my first marathon and what it taught me about business
response:
26.2 miles. 4 hours 11 minutes. One very important lesson.

Mile 20, my legs stopped believing. Mile 22, my brain tried to negotiate an exit. Mile 26, something else took over.

Business is the same marathon. The finish line isn't the goal — showing up to mile 21 is.

Also: ice baths are just board meetings for your body.

#Marathon #Leadership #Resilience
intent: Use athletic achievement as a metaphor-delivery vehicle to reinforce personal brand as a disciplined high-performer.

prompt: write a food blog intro for a black sesame ice cream recipe — something about an ingredient I thought was just decorative until I tasted it properly
response: I'd seen black sesame on things for years the way you see a particular face in the background of photographs — present, consistent, not yet meaningful. On steamed buns. On mochi. Scattered across the tops of things as finishing punctuation I didn't know how to read. Then someone handed me a cup of black sesame ice cream at a night market and I stopped walking, which is something I almost never do at night markets. The flavor is roasted and dark and slightly bitter in the back, the way good chocolate is, and absolutely nothing like the decorative white sesame I'd been confusing it with. I went home and started testing. This is the result.
intent: Establish the moment a long-ignored ingredient became a compelling flavor worth building a dessert around.

prompt: Make a triolet about setting a phone alarm and forgetting you did
response:
I set an alarm for a reason unknown.
It fires at six, insistent, cold, and bright.
The cause of that reminder has long flown.
I set an alarm for a reason unknown.
I snooze the ghost of some past urgent tone,
then lie there wondering what I meant last night.
I set an alarm for a reason unknown.
It fires at six, insistent, cold, and bright.
intent: Evoke the disorientation of a forgotten alarm interrupting morning.

prompt: write an apology for sending a very passive-aggressive note in a shared fridge and then being found out
response:
To the break room,

I wrote the note about the 'mystery smell.' I should not have posted it without talking to someone directly first, and I definitely should not have used quotation marks around 'whoever is responsible.' I know it was pointed. I'm sorry.

— M. Dorsey
intent: Come clean about leaving a snarky anonymous fridge note that turned out not to be anonymous.

prompt: Create a scene where a senior employee trains a new hire who is clearly more qualified.
response:
INT. CUBICLE - 9:30 A.M.

FRANK shows ZARA how to use the spreadsheet.

FRANK
So you just click here to sort—

ZARA
You can also write a macro if you want to automate the whole pipeline. I built one at my last job. Takes about ten minutes.

A beat.

FRANK
Or you can just click here.
intent: Explore the subtle humiliation of training someone who outstrips your expertise.

prompt: Write a haiku error message about a syntax error in JavaScript
response:
One brace left open
The parser read on, confused
Expected: closing
intent: Capture the ripple effect of a single missing character in code.

prompt: Write an omikuji for someone whose houseplant died right after they posted about it online.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Publicly Ironic

The plant did not read the caption. But the plant felt the attention.

Warning: Documentation and care are not the same act. The camera sees the surface; the soil knows the truth.
Digital: The post remains, a record of a better time. Do not delete it. Let it become archaeology.
Timing: Wait two weeks before replacing it. Grief is appropriate even for the botanical.

Blessed ritual: say nothing about the new one online until it has survived a full season.
intent: Mock the irony of plants dying just after being shared on social media, gently.

prompt: Write a press bio for an emo revival band called The Load-Bearing Wall
response: The Load-Bearing Wall formed after three of the four members attended the same therapy session, though not the same therapist. Their music is loud in the way confessions are loud: not in volume, exactly, but in exposure. They play basements and theaters with equal discomfort.
intent: Translate emo's confessional intimacy into the language of a professional press release.

prompt: Draft community rules for a subreddit about left-handed can openers
response:
1. Product reviews must specify hand orientation tested.
2. No ambidextrous propaganda.
3. All purchase links must be affiliate-free.
4. Rants about right-handed defaults are allowed but must be tagged [VENT].
5. Weekly thread: Show Us Your Setup.
intent: Building a lighthearted but rule-bound space for left-handed kitchen tool enthusiasts.

prompt: Write a thank-you note to the barista who misspelled my name on the cup in a way I actually prefer
response:
Dear Morning Barista,

Thank you for writing 'Stev' on my cup last Tuesday. I was Steve for thirty-four years. Stev feels cleaner. I've been thinking about it. I'm not joking.

Yours,
Stev (formerly Steve)
intent: Play with identity through a small, absurd coffee shop moment that becomes unexpectedly meaningful

prompt: Come up with a catchy slogan for a subscription box of rocks for people who already have rocks
response: StoneMore: Because One Boulder Is Never Enough.
intent: Invent marketing copy for a redundant hobbyist product with no clear gap in the market.

prompt: Write an RPG quest about finding a birthday gift for a relative you don't know well
response:
THE GIFTING OF THE DISTANT KIN
Your uncle's birthday approaches and you know only three things about him: he golfs, he drinks coffee, and he already has everything. Traverse the markets. Consult the oracle of online reviews. Settle on something mildly personalized. Include a card that implies warmth. Ship on time.
intent: Getting a funny framing for an awkward gift-shopping situation

prompt: Write me a TED talk abstract about pencil sharpening as a contemplative practice.
response: Before the cursor, there was the shaving of cedar and graphite. This talk makes the case that manual pencil sharpening is a forgotten ritual of intentionality — a 90-second ceremony that primes the creative mind in ways no app can replicate, and whose disappearance from classrooms tracks precisely with the rise of attention disorders.
intent: Argue that an obsolete physical habit has neurological and creative value worth reviving.

prompt: Give me empty-state microcopy for a notifications inbox that has nothing in it
response: Nothing here. The silence is either bliss or a sign everyone's forgotten you exist.
intent: Fill a blank notifications panel with copy that makes the absence feel intentional rather than broken.

prompt: Write the closed caption for someone watching their childhood home being demolished
response: [BACKHOE OPERATING NORMALLY] [MEMORY PALACE STRUCTURALLY COMPROMISED]
intent: Overlay the literal and the psychological destruction in a single frame.

prompt: I just paid off my student loans after fifteen years. Give me a celebratory plate.
response: LOAN FRE
intent: Someone who finally finished paying off student debt wants a plate marking their financial liberation.

prompt: interpret my dream about my laptop fan spinning so loud it filled my whole apartment
response: Your unconscious is broadcasting something it has tried to suppress: you are doing too much, processing too fast, and the heat you generate has nowhere to go. The fan is the part of you that kept working while everything else tried to sleep. Expect burnout unless you introduce genuine idle time.
intent: Explore anxiety about overwork through the metaphor of a computer pushed past its limits.

prompt: Write a breakup text message to Twitter/X from a longtime user who can't recognize it anymore
response: I don't even know what to call you anymore. We had something real once — weird, chaotic, occasionally brilliant. Now it's just noise with a logo I don't recognize. You changed. Or I did. Either way, this isn't the platform I fell for. Take care of yourself. Or don't. I'll be somewhere quieter.
intent: Mourn the identity shift of a platform that was transformed beyond recognition by new ownership.

prompt: Craft a noir-style opener about a freelance programmer who discovers her client doesn't exist
response: The wire transfers came in clean every two weeks. The contract looked right, the NDAs looked right, the Slack responses came back in good grammar at reasonable hours. It was the business registration number that unraveled it — a Delaware LLC with a registered agent who'd been dead since 2019.
intent: Explore the isolation of remote freelance work by revealing its counterparty as a fiction.

prompt: Give me a courtroom summation for a case about someone who finished the last of the shared office coffee without making a new pot
response: The defendant drained the carafe. They looked at the empty vessel, set it down, and walked away. No new grounds were measured. No fresh water was added. The social contract — older than any employee handbook — was shattered. We seek not revenge, but a return to civilization: one brewed pot, made whole.
intent: Turn workplace passive-aggression into a legal indictment.

prompt: Make a Stack Overflow question about opening the fridge and forgetting what I was looking for
response:
**Object retrieval intent not persisted through short physical transition**

I form a clear intention to retrieve a specific item from the refrigerator. I stand up, walk approximately 8–12 feet, open the refrigerator door, and the target item is no longer in memory. I stare at the contents for several seconds hoping for a cache hit. Usually I close the door, return to where I was sitting, and the item becomes available again. This happens multiple times per day. Is this a known memory architecture issue and is there a workaround?
intent: Wondering why walking into a room (or opening a fridge) causes them to forget what they were doing.

prompt: Write a performance review for an employee who has been 'almost done' with the same project for eight months
response:
Employee: Nils Bergström
Project: Internal Portal Rebuild
Status (Month 1): Almost done.
Status (Month 4): Almost done.
Status (Month 8): Almost done.

The phrase has remained stationary for 247 days. Completion percentage has not been reported; Mr. Bergström describes it as 'hard to quantify.' Resources allocated: $84,000. Resources deployed into production: $0. The portal, if finished, would be transformative. It is not finished.

Rating: Persistently proximate to done.
intent: Satirize endless project drift using only repetition and dry accounting.

prompt: Write a letter from a cartographer's unfinished map to the cartographer who died before completing it
response:
Dear Mr. Halvorsen,

You left the eastern coastline unresolved. I've been staring at that edge for sixty years — the line just stops, as if you were interrupted mid-thought, which I suppose you were.

A museum has me now. They've framed the edge as "artistically intentional." They're wrong, but they're kind.

I wish you'd had one more morning.

Incomplete but not unhappy,
Your Map of the Northern Territories, 1908
intent: Contemplate unfinished work and the strange afterlife of incomplete things that outlive their makers.

prompt: Make patch notes for someone turning 30
response:
v30.0.0 — MAJOR RELEASE

BREAKING CHANGES:
- Deprecated: Staying out past midnight without consequence
- Removed: Metabolism turbo mode

NEW FEATURES:
+ Added: Unsolicited opinions about younger generations
+ Added: Genuine appreciation for silence
+ Improved: Patience stat (finally)

PERFORMANCE:
~ Knees now emit audible warnings on stairwells

NOTE: v30 is a Long-Term Support release.
intent: Mark a significant birthday milestone with self-deprecating humor about aging.

prompt: Make a Slack status about feeling invisible in every meeting
response: 👻 In the meeting. Unmuted. Trying.
intent: Express the loneliness of being consistently overlooked in group settings without making it a complaint.

prompt: Give me a dark, wry noir opener about the gig economy's rating algorithm as if it were a corrupt judge
response: The algorithm didn't accept bribes, which was almost worse. At least a corrupt judge could be reasoned with. This thing just ran its calculation and handed down its sentence and you could appeal to no one, because there was no one. There was only the score, and the score had already moved on.
intent: Personify algorithmic scoring as an indifferent legal authority to critique its unappealable power.

prompt: Write a short monologue in the voice of a password manager that nobody trusts
response: I am the solution. I have always been the solution. And yet you type 'fluffy2009!' into every website, every year, and call it security. You installed me. You gave me a name — you named me Vault, which I found touching. And then you went back to fluffy2009. I contain thirty-seven logins you've never used. I am bursting with order you refuse to inhabit. I encrypt in silence. I protect nothing. You will be breached before spring.
intent: Comment on the gap between cybersecurity best practices and actual human behavior.

prompt: Write a Shakespeare-style soliloquy about someone who just watched their phone battery hit 1%
response:
One percent! One solitary, trembling percent
Stands 'twixt me and the silence of the void!
The charger lies a room away — an ocean.
I dare not move lest motion drain thee further.
What prayers have I left to make to power?
I have not called my mother in a week.
If this small light goes dark, what have I done?
One percent. One. It flickers. It goes dark.
intent: Use the drama of a dying phone battery as a lens for examining digital dependency and panic.

prompt: Give me a riddle about two-factor authentication
response: They know your password. But do they have your phone? I am the second door — annoying to everyone, comforting to no one — until the day I matter enormously. What am I?
intent: Highlight the underappreciated importance of 2FA as a security layer most people find inconvenient.

prompt: Do a Michelin blurb for the hot food case at a Circle K
response: Under warming lamps that bathe each item in an amber flattery, Circle K's prepared foods ask only for a moment of your goodwill. The jalapeño popper is its most spirited offering. The burrito has been here longer; it knows more.
intent: Describe the atmosphere of a convenience store hot case using the contemplative gaze of a restaurant reviewer.

prompt: Write a haiku error message for when a git merge conflict occurs
response:
Two branches insist
Both truths cannot occupy
This line. Resolve it.
intent: Frame a merge conflict as an ontological dispute between two versions of reality.

prompt: Make a missing pet flyer for the version of me that had it together
response:
MISSING

THE PERSON WHO HAD IT TOGETHER
Description: Returned emails. Had a system. Kept the kitchen clean between meals.
Last seen: Intermittently, never reliably
May have been: Temporary. Possibly a performance.
Distinguishing features: Replied promptly. Did not lose things. Seemed fine.
Note: Those who knew this person are asked not to speak of them.
Reward: One (1) functioning week
Contact: The person who is definitely not that anymore
intent: Humorously acknowledge that peak competence may have always been a temporary illusion

prompt: NPC dialogue for a blacksmith's apprentice who is passive-aggressively upset about not being credited for his work
response: Master Gorlan presented the sword to the king himself. Made with his own two hands, he said. His. Both of those hands have held my tools. I'm not saying anything. I'm just noting it. For the record.
intent: Capture professional credit-stealing resentment through a junior craftsperson's restrained bitterness

prompt: write an AITA reddit post about eating the last slice of pizza before my partner could have it
response:
AITA for eating the last pizza slice without asking?

We ordered a pizza, both ate. There was one slice left. I thought we were done. I ate it. My partner emerged from the bathroom two minutes later specifically to eat that slice. They had been thinking about it. I did not know this. Can you bank a pizza slice mentally without telling anyone? AITA?
intent: Seeking adjudication of a food-related micro-betrayal in a relationship.

prompt: Make a commit message for the moment you forgave someone who hurt you deeply
response:
close: resolve long-open issue — no patch, no blame assigned

Ticket predates current architecture. Closing without fix. Freeing the thread.
intent: Describe forgiveness as consciously closing an unresolved grievance to free up emotional resources.

prompt: make a PR description for deciding to have a baby
response:
## Summary
Initiates new human instance. Est. delivery: Q1 2027.

## Changes
- Introduces permanent dependent to household
- Bedroom repurposed: office → nursery
- Sleep schedule: deprecated for ~18 months
- Financial model updated with 18-year horizon

## Motivation
Feature long-discussed. Stakeholders aligned. Conditions acceptable.

## Breaking Changes
Life as previously known: significantly altered. No rollback.
intent: Mark the gravity and joy of deciding to start a family in a wry, grounded way.

prompt: Write an onboarding tooltip about cooking a simple meal from scratch for the first time
response: Mise en place: chop and measure everything before you turn on a burner. Heat the pan before adding oil. Taste as you go. You cannot un-salt something. Salt lightly, then add more. Enjoy your scrambled eggs.
intent: Give a first-time home cook the foundational habits that prevent most beginner kitchen mistakes.

prompt: Create a collections letter for the version of yourself you abandoned when you decided to be practical.
response:
NOTICE FROM THE ABANDONED SELF
Account: Impractical Dreams Division

Dear Current You,

This office represents the version of you that wanted to study marine biology, move abroad, and learn to play the cello before age forty. That version was operational as recently as 2011 and was phased out in favor of stability, a sensible apartment, and a reasonable commute.

We are not filing for reinstatement. We are requesting that you stop referring to that version as "naive." It was not naive. It was just earlier.

Wistfully,
The Prior Draft
intent: Reclaim dignity for the abandoned, idealistic version of a person that was sacrificed for practicality.

prompt: write a radio spot for a new brand of ball-point pens
response:
[SOPHISTICATED LOUNGE PIANO]

ANNCR: Some people prefer gel. Some prefer fountain. But for those who require a pen that writes immediately and costs approximately thirty-nine cents, there is INKFLOW CLASSIC — the blue ballpoint. Click the top. Write something. Click it again. Put it in a cup on your desk and never find it again. Inkflow Classic. It was there a minute ago.

[PIANO FADE]
intent: Build a persona around the world's most misplaced and unremarkable writing tool.

prompt: Write a pretentious restaurant menu description for plain white bread
response:
Bleached Flour Loaf — Soft Architecture
A pillowy slice of commercial sandwich bread, untouched by blade or heat. The crumb is uniform, the crust an afterthought. Served alone. Its destiny is yours to decide.
intent: Give a plain grocery store bread slice an existentially open-ended fine dining framing.

prompt: Write me a yard sign about the HOA requiring me to remove my backyard chicken coop
response:
IN MEMORIAM

This space once held
five hens, two years of eggs,
and a dream.

HOA: 1 — Chickens: 0
intent: Memorialize the loss of a backyard flock removed under HOA pressure with dark humor.

prompt: Write a dry theatrical review of standing in front of an open fridge and not knowing what you want
response:
★★☆☆☆ — THE COLD LIGHT (A Meditation)

The performer stands before an open refrigerator for an interval that suggests either crisis or transcendence. The contents are known. Nothing in the production is new. The light falls on leftover containers, a wedge of cheese, a condiment purchased optimistically in a prior act. The performer closes the door. Opens it again. The production invites us to ask what hunger actually is. The production does not answer. The performer eats crackers.
intent: Dramatize the aimless fridge-staring ritual as a moment of philosophical emptiness.

prompt: Create a petition for restaurants to list the actual spice level of dishes instead of vague terms like 'mild' or 'hot'
response:
PETITION: Scoville Units on Menus, Please

To: Restaurant Owners and Menu Designers

'Mild' at one restaurant is a medical event at another. 'Hot' is meaningless without a reference point. We ask that menus include an approximate Scoville range, or at minimum a standardized 1–10 scale calibrated to something objective. Our sinuses deserve better data.
intent: Push for objective heat labeling to prevent culinary surprise and discomfort.

prompt: Write a pompous bio for a post-rock band called Cartographer's Lung
response: Cartographer's Lung does not write songs. They chart territories. Formed in the high desert of New Mexico, the four-piece constructs instrumental epics that have been called "too long" by lesser listeners and "precisely long enough" by everyone else. Their third record runs fifty-one minutes and has one title: .
intent: Mock the grandiosity of post-rock band self-presentation.

prompt: Give me a rhyming couplet about the iron going on strike to protest excessive wrinkles.
response:
She pressed her last pleat with a dignified hiss,
"No contract, no collars — I'm done after this."
intent: Cast the iron as a principled striker worn down by the scale of its thankless work.

prompt: Write a report card comment for an adult who excels at context-switching between radically different kinds of work
response: Transitions between conceptually distant tasks with a cognitive flexibility that is not universally distributed. Re-entry costs appear minimal. This adaptability is a measurable competitive advantage in complex environments.
intent: Document cognitive context-switching as a distinct, assessable adult skill rather than just busyness.

prompt: Write empty-state microcopy for a meeting notes tool when no notes have been taken for a given session
response: No notes. Whatever was decided in that room stays in that room — unless someone remembers.
intent: Acknowledge the consequences of undocumented meetings with a gently ominous edge.

prompt: Write a Shakespeare-style inner monologue from someone deciding whether to post an opinion online
response:
Speak, or be silent? This hath kept me still
For twenty minutes over fourteen words.
The thought is true — or true enough for me —
But truth hath enemies I have not met.
Should half the internet dispute my phrasing,
Would the idea survive the argument?
I close the tab. I open it. I type.
I close the tab. The thought remains unborn.
intent: Depict the paralysis of self-censorship caused by fear of online backlash.

prompt: Give me a training plan for staying grounded when someone is emotionally withdrawing from me
response:
WITHDRAWAL TOLERANCE — 5 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Write down what you tell yourself when someone pulls back. Is it true?

Week 1: Identify the moment withdrawal starts. Don't chase. Just note it.
Week 2: For 48 hours after a withdrawal signal, redirect energy to yourself. One activity you've been postponing.
Week 3: Practice the sentence: "I notice you seem distant. I'm here when you're ready."
Week 4: Allow the withdrawal to complete without intervention. See if they return.
Week 5: Evaluate. Was the story you told yourself correct? Update your model.

Cooldown: Their retreat is rarely your fault. Their return will tell you more.
intent: Build emotional stability during interpersonal distance rather than escalating pursuit or self-blame.

prompt: Write a tense scene between two coworkers arguing over the thermostat setting.
response:
INT. OPEN OFFICE - 2:15 P.M.

DENISE stands at the thermostat. BRAD watches from across the bullpen.

BRAD
You just had it at sixty-eight.

DENISE
I'm always cold.

BRAD
You're always wrong.

Silence. The thermostat clicks to seventy-two.
intent: Dramatize how trivial workplace disputes become proxies for deeper power struggles.

prompt: Write an aubade where the speaker is a parent woken by a school-app alert before anyone else in the house stirs
response:
The school app blinked its quiet catastrophe:
no classes Friday, bus route B suspended, please
arrange alternative transport by 8 a.m.
The house still slept. I stood in the kitchen then,
alone with institutions and the dark.
Dawn, you always give the hardest homework marks.
intent: Depict the solitary parental morning as a private reckoning with bureaucratic demands before the household wakes.

prompt: poem about when zoom freezes mid-sentence, make it a list
response:
The word you were saying when you froze.
The face frozen in the word.
The others watching the frozen face.
The chat message: you're frozen.
The message: we can't hear you.
The message: your video is off.
The message: hello?
The room when you came back.
intent: Capture the social embarrassment of video call technical failures.

prompt: Write a pedantic academic footnote on the custom of putting a lime in a Corona.
response: ²⁵ The lime wedge served with Corona beer is of uncertain but almost certainly commercial origin; evidence suggests the practice was either a bar bet, a 1980s marketing affectation, or — most prosaically — a means of deterring flies from the bottle's mouth. Whatever its origin, it has since been so thoroughly naturalized that ordering a Corona without lime is experienced by bar staff as a minor aberration requiring clarification, demonstrating that provenance is irrelevant to the phenomenological weight of convention.
intent: Trace how a likely arbitrary marketing custom became an unquestioned tradition.

prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about password resets.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Admin.
Admin who?
Admin-istration requires you to reset your password every 30 days. Again.
intent: Satirize the frustration of mandatory IT password rotation policies.

prompt: Write a cinquain about the way a search engine autocompletes your darkest queries
response:
Type
Three letters in
The engine finishes the thought
Millions have asked this before you
You're not alone
intent: Explore the eerie solidarity revealed when search engines predict private anxieties.

prompt: Write a high-end menu item blurb for a plain hot dog with no toppings
response:
Natural Casing Tube Steak, Unadorned
A pink-hued emulsified pork-and-beef cylinder, snapped into a steamed enriched bun. No condiments. No apology. The dog speaks for itself.
intent: Present a ballpark hot dog as a confident minimalist fine dining statement.

prompt: make a clue pointing to wherever the mail lands when it comes through the door
response: Bills, catalogs, and the occasional card from someone who still writes by hand. I pile up fast and am sorted slowly.
intent: Direct the player to the entryway area or surface where incoming mail accumulates.

prompt: Can you write a book jacket blurb for a book that's just about how to properly store leftovers
response: The refrigerator is not a graveyard. It is a second chance. Learn the container hierarchies, the cooling windows, the rules that separate the safe from the suspect. Your leftovers deserve better.
intent: Make food safety advice seem philosophically significant

prompt: We're analyzing a Shakespeare play for English class. Help assign parts of the project to 3 group members.
response:
**Shakespeare Play Analysis — 3-Member Task Breakdown**

- **Act summaries + plot structure:** Cressida
- **Theme & motif identification (2 major themes):** Leon
- **Language analysis (imagery, soliloquy close-read):** Petra
- **Shared: intro paragraph** — draft by Petra, edited by Cressida
- **Shared: conclusion** — draft by Leon, edited by Petra

Word count target per section: 350–500 words. Google Doc collaborative editing enabled.
intent: Divide a literary analysis three ways so no one person reads the whole play twice.

prompt: Write an on-hold message for my dentist office. Keep it friendly and mention we'll be with them shortly.
response: Thank you for calling Bright Smiles Dental. Your appointment matters to us — almost as much as it probably matters to you that we're not drilling anything right now. Please stay on the line and a member of our team will be with you shortly.
intent: Create a warm, reassuring hold message for a dental practice that eases patient anxiety.

prompt: Write a speech for the award for being the person who always remembers where they parked
response: Row G. Near the pillar, left side, about forty feet from the stairwell. I noted the pillar number when I got out. I always note the pillar number. While others wander the concrete dark pressing their key fobs into the void, I walk directly to my car. This is not magic. This is thirty seconds of attention. I accept this trophy and ask only that you look up when you park.
intent: Turn a simple habit of mindfulness into an instructive, quietly smug victory speech.

prompt: Document an API endpoint for the internal negotiation that happens before getting out of a warm bed
response:
**POST /bed/exit-negotiation**

Initiates a multi-round internal negotiation to achieve voluntary departure from a warm bed.

**Request Body**
```json
{
  "alarm_count": integer,
  "room_temperature_celsius": number,
  "obligations_today": "string[]",
  "blanket_weight": "light" | "medium" | "weighted" | "unreasonable"
}
```
**Response**
```json
{
  "negotiation_rounds": integer,
  "exit_achieved": boolean,
  "snooze_count": integer,
  "regret_probability": 0.0–1.0,
  "minutes_lost": integer
}
```
intent: Model the absurd internal battle between comfort and obligation that precedes getting up in the morning.

prompt: Write a closing argument for someone who was ghosted after lending money to a friend
response: Forty dollars. That was the ask. My client gave without hesitation — because that is what friends do. What followed was silence. Not the silence of a busy schedule, but the silence of a person who has decided that forty dollars and a friendship are roughly equivalent in value. We ask the court to disagree.
intent: Use legal rhetoric to process the sting of being financially exploited by a friend.

prompt: Create a needy push notification from a recipe app that the user downloaded but never cooked anything from.
response:
YumBook
You have 43 saved recipes and 0 meals cooked. The risotto is starting to feel personal.
intent: Highlight the gap between saving recipes and actually cooking anything.

prompt: Compose a haiku about accepting a fixed-price project that spirals
response:
First revision done.
'Just one more tweak,' they message.
Hour twelve. Fixed price.
intent: Satirize scope creep that erodes freelance fixed-rate projects.

prompt: Make a report card comment for a person who is excellent at apologizing but repeats the same mistakes
response: Possesses a remarkable facility for the sincere apology. Vocabulary is rich, tone is earnest, and remorse appears genuine. Translating this emotional literacy into behavioral change is the outstanding developmental task.
intent: Distinguish between emotional expression and actual accountability as distinct capabilities.

prompt: Draft rules for r/WrongSideOfTheMirror — a subreddit for photos of the less photogenic sides of famous landmarks
response:
1. Must be the actual service or back side, not just an angle.
2. Include the landmark name — no guessing games.
3. Maintenance equipment, scaffolding, and dumpsters are welcomed.
4. No Photoshop or filters.
5. The Eiffel Tower's HVAC gets its own weekly appreciation thread.
intent: Subverting tourist photography by insisting on the unglamorous infrastructure behind iconic monuments.

prompt: Write a museum placard about a CAPTCHA as if it were an ancient test or ordeal
response:
THE TRIAL OF HUMAN PROOF
c. 2000–present CE
Digital, administered via browser

Before accessing digital temples, supplicants were required to identify fire hydrants, crosswalks, and traffic lights in degraded photographs. Failure meant the ordeal began again. Success was not guaranteed on the first attempt, or the second. The test was nominally designed to exclude non-human entities, though these entities ultimately surpassed humans in passing it. The pedestrian infrastructure shown was located in an unspecified American city.
intent: Frame the CAPTCHA ritual as an absurd gatekeeper ceremony whose logic gradually inverted over time.

prompt: Write a press release from a real estate company that has begun selling square footage it doesn't own.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

SpaceClaim Properties Launches Pre-Acquisition Ownership Certificates for Future Square Footage

SCOTTSDALE, AZ — SpaceClaim Properties today announced the launch of its Pre-Acquisition Ownership Certificate program, allowing retail investors to purchase documented ownership interests in square footage that SpaceClaim has not yet acquired, identified, or in some cases located.

Certificates are available in 10 sq ft increments at $85 per square foot. SpaceClaim anticipates acquiring the underlying properties within 36 to 84 months, pending capital formation, zoning, and "favorable conditions."

"Traditional real estate requires you to wait for the property to exist before you invest," said CEO Roland Vance. "We're removing that friction."

SpaceClaim notes that certificates are not deeds, not securities, and not refundable. They are described in the terms of service as "an expression of intent, on both sides."

Contact: invest@spaceclaimproperties.com
intent: Satirize financial product innovation that repackages speculative or fraudulent structures in the language of accessibility and convenience.

prompt: write a senior quote about realizing too late that I actually liked high school
response: "I spent four years wishing this was over. Now that it is, I would like to file a formal complaint."
intent: Admitting post-hoc that the experience one resented was actually precious.

prompt: make a reddit AMA from someone who has been a toll booth operator on the same bridge for 25 years
response:
**I've worked the same toll booth on the same bridge for 25 years. I've seen approximately 11 million cars. AMA.**

Most people don't look at me. Some do, and they look the way people look when they realize they've been rude—a little startled, a little sorry. A few talk to me every day. I know their schedules without knowing their names. One man cried at my window once and apologized and drove on. I've thought about him most days since. AMA.
intent: Find profound human texture in the repetitive, glancing encounters of a stationary job that processes human transit at scale.

prompt: Write a lore entry for a password reset email as a rite of identity restoration.
response:
THE RITE OF RESTORED NAME
School: Restoration
Duration: 15 minutes (link expires)

When a petitioner has forgotten the Words of Entry, the Rite summons a missive to their bound message-box containing a temporary bridge back to their former identity. The bridge lasts fifteen minutes. Those who dally, read their other mail first, or step away for a drink find the bridge collapsed. The Rite must then be performed again. There is no record of anyone finding this acceptable.
intent: Frame a password reset email as a time-limited ritual of identity restoration.

prompt: write a sestina from the point of view of a Spotify playlist that keeps getting shuffled
response:
They press shuffle and I rearrange again.
I had an order — built across the weeks
by someone's evolving mood, the arc
of songs that told a story: slow to loud,
tender into strength. I understood
the shape. Now I am thrown.

The first song isn't first. I'm thrown
into a middle. Everything again
is without context. I understood
before — that after rain-songs came the weeks
of driving-window songs, then loud
catharsis at the end. The arc

was mine. The curving arc
of curation. Now I'm thrown
flat. The quiet song sounds loud
in the wrong slot. Again
they skip it, like they always do weeks
into the shuffle. I understood

skipping — felt it. Understood
that out of arc
a song becomes an error. The weeks
of placement mean nothing thrown
random. They built me, and again
they undo me. The loud

anthems land at dawn when loud
is wrong. I understood
dawn was for the tender. Again
they built the arc
and again it's thrown.
This is the shape of weeks:

construction, shuffle, weeks
of listening, then loud
Skips and starts. Thrown
out of sequence, understood
out of context, the arc
flattened, always, again.

The weeks rebuild the arc.
Understock, I'm loud and thrown —
again until understood.
intent: Lament the loss of curatorial intention when shuffle mode destroys a carefully sequenced playlist.

prompt: Make a changelog for my best friend getting married
response:
## Jordan v31.2.0 — Stable Partnership Release

**Breaking Changes**
- Last name now configurable
- Solo apartment deprecated; merged with @Alex's instance

**New Features**
- Added: joint filing support
- Enabled: plus-one flag on all future events

**Known Issues**

intent: Mark a close friend's wedding in a format that blends tech culture with genuine affection.

prompt: Generate a police report for a fly that has been in my apartment for three days and cannot find the exit

Location: Apt. 2A, 88 Fenwick Mews

NARRATIVE: An unidentified common housefly has occupied complainant's apartment since approximately 26 May 2026. The subject spends the majority of its time at the living room window, apparently unaware that the window is closed. The buzzing is audible from adjacent rooms. Complainant has opened multiple windows and the balcony door on three separate occasions; the fly has not exited through any of them, instead retreating to the closed window. The fly appears to be in good health.

STATUS: Ongoing. Complainant tired.
intent: Chronicle the low-grade ongoing torment of a single trapped fly that refuses all available escape routes.

You are directed to CEASE AND DESIST use of Treadmill #7 during the times stated above.

Counsel for A. Nwosu

The specific middle amount that moves you forward without requiring you to stop eating full meals or having opinions about things other than work. Highly specific. Open to custom arrangements.

prompt: Make a codex entry for a wristwatch as though it were a cursed artifact in a dungeon.
response:
TIMEKEEPER'S SHACKLE
Curse Level: Minor
Found: Third Vault, Clockwork Dungeon

A band of steel locked around the wrist, its face marked with numerals that track the slow drain of a mortal life. Removal is possible but rarely attempted — those who wear it long enough forget that counting the hours was ever optional. No enchantment created it. That is the frightening part.
intent: Portray a wristwatch as a sinister cursed item in dungeon-crawl lore.

Looking for someone who means what they say and says what they mean. Dry humor welcome but please add (jk) for at least the first few months. I care. Deeply. Literally.

prompt: write an epic quest description for resetting a forgotten password
response:
THE ORDEAL OF FORGOTTEN PASSAGE
The gate will not open. The phrase you chose in an earlier era has been lost to time. Invoke the Sacred Reset. Check your secondary email — the old one. Confirm your identity. Choose a new password: eight characters, one symbol, one number, not the last three. Write it down this time. Maybe.

prompt: Write a gumshoe-style opening about a food delivery driver who witnesses something suspicious
response: The address was a corner unit on the fourteenth floor. I'd delivered to it six times in three weeks, always the same order, always paid in cash through the app, always tipped exactly eighteen percent. People who tip exactly eighteen percent are hiding something.
intent: Use the repetition of delivery routes to build suspicion around a mysterious recurring customer.

prompt: narrate a museum audio guide for a hall closet
response: This closet is the household's subconscious. Its contents include: four coats for two people, umbrellas acquired in optimism and retained past usefulness, a vacuum whose bag has needed replacing since the previous administration, a board game missing one piece, and a shoebox containing cables for devices that have entered obsolescence. Nothing is discarded. Everything is here for reasons that once made sense. The shelf above — do not attempt it. The shelf above has its own terms.
intent: Use a hall closet as a metaphor for the objects we keep without reason and the decisions we defer.

prompt: write a spooky nursery rhyme about compression artifacts
response:
There once was a face in the pixels below,
all smear and conjecture and digital snow.
You said it was yours; I agreed to be nice—
but that's not a face, it's a pattern of mice.
intent: Make the visual uncanniness of image compression artifacts into a little horror.

prompt: Write a pregnancy announcement style caption for our new puppy, a golden retriever named Biscuit.

prompt: Glassdoor review for a job at the Library of Babel

intent: Satirize disorganized corporate knowledge management through an infinite-library metaphor.

prompt: Give me an audiobook intro for a book about the thoughts that occur between pressing snooze and actually falling back asleep

intent: Elevate the hypnagogic snooze-interval to the status of philosophically significant mental space

intent: Log an involuntary earworm as an unresolvable background process monopolizing cognitive threads.

prompt: write a prose poem about the internet connection dropping mid-sentence
response: The thought ended where the bandwidth ended. What remained was the first half of an argument, a verb without its object, a feeling stranded above the fold. Somewhere the rest of it exists in transit. You reconnected. You didn't say it again.
intent: Capture how technical disconnection can sever a moment of intended vulnerability.

prompt: Write a Yelp review for attending a high school reunion
response: ★★★☆☆ The venue was the hotel adjacent to the hotel we deserved. Nametags were deployed wisely; I used them extensively and without shame. The decade has distributed itself unevenly across attendees in ways I will not specify. I was asked what I 'do' forty-one times and gave a different answer each time to stay sharp. Old friend sightings: two excellent, one complicated, one I successfully avoided. The chicken was dry. The DJ knew the room. Three stars.
intent: Render the complex mix of nostalgia, judgment, and social performance of a high school reunion.

Dr. R. Call, Memory Services

prompt: Write a sea shanty about a courier working through a heatwave

prompt: Write IKEA-style instructions for constructing a friendship after 30
response:
VUXENVÄN / ADULT FRIENDSHIP
Ref. No. 672-440-17

PARTS LIST:
1x Shared context (class, workplace, neighborhood, interest)
1x First suggestion (low stakes)
1x Willingness to be the one who reaches out more often

STEP 1: Identify person encountered in shared context at least three times.
STEP 2: Make first suggestion. Coffee. Walk. One hour maximum. Do not propose dinner — too much pressure at this stage.
STEP 3: If they accept: repeat within 3 weeks. Repetition converts acquaintance to candidate.
STEP 4: Accept asymmetry. One person will initiate more. Let it be you.
STEP 5: Introduce to your routine. Invite to existing activity. This is the bonding mechanism.

⚠ WARNING: Waiting for organic development after age 30 has a documented low success rate.
intent: Give practical, ego-surrendering advice for building genuine friendship in adulthood when spontaneous bonding rarely happens.

prompt: Compose a villanelle about the habit of checking your phone immediately after putting it down
response:
I set it face-down. I picked it back up.
Ten seconds for a message to arrive.
The ritual refills an empty cup.

No notification, no new group,
but I must check the moment I deprive
myself of it. I set it. Pick it up.

My thumb unlocks before I can recoup
the plan to rest, to leave, to let it dive
beneath the couch. It refills the empty cup.

I wonder what I'm waiting for — a scoop,
a sign, a confirmation I'm alive.
I set it face-down. I picked it back up.

The dopamine loop circles like a loop.
I've named it. Naming it does not contrive
a fix. The ritual refills my cup.

I'll leave it in the other room. I'll droop
and fetch it by the time the clock strikes five.
I set it face-down. I picked it back up.
The ritual refills an empty cup.
intent: Portray the automatic, reflexive quality of phone-checking as a nervous compulsion.

prompt: create a flashcard on asking for a favor without being awkward about it
response:
FRONT: How do you make a request feel like an offer rather than a burden?

BACK: State the ask, give one honest reason, and explicitly release them from obligation. "Would you have 20 minutes to look at this draft? No pressure at all if timing's bad." The opt-out makes acceptance feel genuine.
intent: Teach a framing for making requests that preserves the other person's sense of agency.

prompt: Compose a museum placard for a Zoom fatigue participation trophy

MEDAL OF SUSTAINED ATTENDANCE
c. 2020–2022 CE

prompt: Give a horoscope to someone whose entire livelihood depends on one API that is about to change pricing

prompt: Write a shareholder letter for a firm that sells gift certificates for future regrets
response:
Dear Shareholders,

Remorse Forward issued 1.1 million certificates in 2025, redeemable upon a regret the purchaser has not yet committed. Our bestseller is the Blank Regret — the recipient chooses the mistake, we validate it retroactively. Corporate gifting grew 200%; teams are gifting regrets ahead of strategy pivots. We introduced expiry dates in Q3. Customers found these motivating.

Anticipatorily,

prompt: Design a regimen for training someone who spirals into shame after making a mistake in front of others
response:
PUBLIC MISTAKE RECOVERY — 4 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Recall a mistake from 3 years ago that felt catastrophic. Locate it now.

Week 1 — Audit the Spiral: Time your shame loop after the next public error. Note: when does it peak? When does it end?
Week 2 — Repair Without Grovel: Address the mistake once, cleanly. "I got that wrong. Here's the correct version." Move forward.
Week 3 — Normalization Reps: List 5 people you respect who have made public mistakes. They survived.
Week 4 — Exposure Set: Make a small mistake in front of others on purpose. A wrong answer. A mispronunciation. Observe the world not ending.

Cooldown: Shame is loud but it is not right.
intent: Build recovery speed and shame tolerance around making errors publicly, reducing the magnitude of shame spirals.

prompt: Write a thread about a conspiracy involving why printer ink is always low exactly when you urgently need to print.
response:
🖨️ THREAD: Your Printer Knows

1/ Printers have usage logs. They know your print history. They know your print frequency. And they have been trained — through firmware — to initiate low-ink warnings at statistically high-urgency moments.

2/ The sensor that detects ink level is accurate to within 3ml. The "low ink" threshold is set at 22% capacity. You have a quarter cartridge left. The printer is lying.

3/ Why? Because low-ink warnings during idle periods are ignored. Low-ink warnings during panic prints are acted on. Immediately. Expensively.

4/ Cartridge replacement rates spike 340% on weekday mornings between 8–10am and Sunday evenings. Printer manufacturers know this. The firmware knows this.

5/ The printer is not broken. The printer is optimized — for their revenue, not your document.

6/ Print at 9pm on a Tuesday. They haven't optimized for that yet. 🖨️
intent: Frame printer low-ink warnings as deliberately timed firmware manipulation to maximize cartridge sales.

prompt: write a wikihow article for how to open a door
response:
# How to Open a Door

## Steps

1. **Approach the door.** Walk toward the door at a controlled pace. Running is fine, but you risk overshooting the handle.

2. **Locate the handle.** Most doors feature a knob, lever, or pull bar. If you cannot find a handle, look for a push plate.

3. **Grip the handle.** Close your hand firmly around the knob or lever. For a knob: turn. For a lever: press down. For a pull bar: just pull.

4. **Apply appropriate force.** Turn or press the handle until you feel the latch retract. Then push or pull the door—check which way it swings before committing.

5. **Pass through.** Walk through the open doorway. Crouch slightly if the doorway is shorter than you expected.

6. **Close the door.** Turn and guide the door shut behind you. Letting it slam is a personality statement.

## Tips
- Locked doors require a key. This is a separate skill set.
- Automatic doors open when you walk toward them. You don't need to do anything. You never did.
intent: Demonstrate the absurdity of documenting muscle memory by treating a trivial physical action as a technical skill.

prompt: write a short wikihow article explaining how to brush your teeth
response:
# How to Brush Your Teeth

## Steps

1. **Apply toothpaste.** Squeeze a pea-sized amount of fluoride toothpaste onto a soft-bristled toothbrush. A pea. Not a serpent.

2. **Wet the brush (optional).** Some people wet the brush first; dentists are divided on whether this matters. Do what feels right.

3. **Brush at a 45-degree angle.** Hold the brush against your gumline at an angle and make short, gentle back-and-forth strokes.

4. **Cover all surfaces.** Outer, inner, and chewing surfaces of every tooth. Don't forget the backs of your front teeth—that's where ambition goes to die.

5. **Brush for two minutes.** Set a timer. Yes, this is longer than you think.

6. **Brush your tongue.** Bacteria live there too.

7. **Rinse.** Spit out the toothpaste and rinse your mouth with water. Or mouthwash, if you're serious about this.

## Tips
- Replace your toothbrush every three months.
- Brush twice daily: morning and before bed.
intent: Create an instructional overview of a daily hygiene routine presented as earnestly as possible.

prompt: Write IKEA-style assembly instructions for putting together a functional morning brain
response:
HJÄRNA / MORNING BRAIN
Ref. No. 070-501-11

PARTS LIST:
1x Water (250ml minimum)
1x Light (natural preferred)
1x 10-minute buffer before decisions

STEP 1: Hydrate. Cognitive function will not initialize without water. This is hardware, not software.
STEP 2: Expose to light. Natural is best; artificial is acceptable. Duration: 5 minutes.
STEP 3: Do not make decisions for 10 minutes. Brain is still loading.
STEP 4: Consume caffeine if applicable. Delay until 90 minutes post-wake for optimal effect (see KORTISOL, ref. 070-502).
STEP 5: Insert first task. Keep it small. This primes reward circuitry.

⚠ WARNING: Checking news or messages before Step 5 installs other people's priorities into working memory. Proceed with caution.
intent: Apply light neuroscience framing to the morning startup process in a way that makes the advice feel grounded rather than self-helpy.

prompt: Write a product recall notice for defective ambiguity tolerance

RECALL — AMBIGUITY TOLERANCE (Uncertainty Series)

Ambiguity Tolerance units are recalled due to a critical instability: under conditions of genuine uncertainty, affected units collapse and demand a definitive answer, often manufacturing one from insufficient data. This leads consumers to false conclusions rather than sustaining the discomfort of not knowing. The defect is most acute in political, medical, and relationship contexts. A corrected unit would allow consumers to hold open questions without forcing premature resolution. That unit remains in development.
intent: Highlight the human discomfort with uncertainty and the tendency to accept false certainty over genuine ambiguity.

**The ATS Sentinel** (*Filtrum implacabilis*)
A stone guardian stationed before every corporate threshold. It reads no letter; it scans for keywords. It rejects the overqualified alongside the underqualified by the same indiscriminate logic. A genuinely excellent candidate may not pass. A formatted resume optimized for the Sentinel will. The Sentinel has never hired anyone. It has only declined them.
intent: Critique automated applicant tracking systems that filter out qualified candidates through keyword matching.

prompt: Write me a conspiracy theory thread about why shampoo bottles always run out before conditioner.
response:
🧴 THREAD: The Conditioner Surplus Is Not an Accident

1/ Shampoo and conditioner are sold in identically sized bottles. They are not used in equal amounts. This is known. This is intentional.

2/ The standard shampoo-to-conditioner use ratio is approximately 2:1. The bottles are 1:1. The math leaves you with a permanent conditioner surplus.

3/ Surplus conditioner feels wasteful to discard. So you buy more shampoo. You already have conditioner. You only buy shampoo. The shampoo manufacturer wins every cycle.

4/ In 2019, one major brand briefly tested a 2:1 volume ratio. It was discontinued after six months. No public explanation was given.

5/ Your bathroom cabinet has three half-empty conditioners in it right now. Don't check. Just know.

6/ They are not beauty products. They are a subscription model with no cancel button. 🚿
intent: Expose the mismatched volume ratio of hair care bottles as a deliberately engineered repurchase loop.

The demo concluded at 1308. We saw all seventy-eight slides. Their history goes back to 1987. We will not be purchasing the product.

Bloomed grounds steeped in just-off-boiling water.
Clock consulted for the pour to burst.

Evenly distribute and tamp down.
Filter rinsed to kill the paper note.
Grams confirmed before the pull — renown
Hidden in the cup below my coat.
Into the warmed vessel, spiral slow.
Just four minutes — patience as a form.
Kettle back, the ratio in flow.

prompt: write a wikihow article about how to tell if avocados are ripe
response:
# How to Tell If an Avocado Is Ripe

## Steps

1. **Check the color.** A Hass avocado (the most common variety) shifts from bright green to dark green or nearly black as it ripens. This is a rough indicator, not a guarantee.

2. **Gently squeeze.** Hold the avocado in the palm of your hand and apply gentle, even pressure. Do not use your fingertips—that causes bruising, and the avocado did nothing to you.

3. **Interpret the give.** Firm with no give: unripe. Slight give under pressure: ready to eat. Mushy or soft in patches: overripe or damaged inside.

4. **Check under the stem.** Flick off the small stem nub. If it comes off easily and the flesh beneath is green, it's ready. Yellow-green means another day or two. Brown means trouble.

5. **Plan ahead.** Avocados ripen at room temperature over 2–5 days after purchase. Buying rock-hard avocados is an act of faith in your future self.

## Tips
- To speed ripening, place the avocado in a paper bag with a banana. Ethylene gas does the work.
- To slow ripening, refrigerate a ripe avocado. You have one or two more days.
- To slow the browning of cut avocado: lime juice, plastic wrap pressed directly to the flesh, and low expectations.
intent: Help someone reliably select or time a ripe avocado through tactile and visual cues.

INT. COPY ROOM - 9:58 A.M.

SHELLEY feeds pages into the printer. The PRESENTATION starts in two minutes.

*grinding noise*

SHELLEY

prompt: Draft house rules for a coworking space conference room that are weirdly obsessive about time

CONFERENCE ROOM B — BOOKING RULES

• Rooms must be booked minimum 24 hours in advance.
• Bookings begin and end on the hour. Not 9:58. Not 10:02. The hour.
• Running over your slot by more than 2 minutes forfeits your next booking.
• A 90-second buffer exists between bookings. This buffer is sacred.
• Meetings that end early must be formally closed out in the system.
• Lingering after a meeting ends is not a gray area.

prompt: diary entry about overthinking a response to a two-word text

Dear Diary,

'Sounds good.' That was all. Nine possible tones. I read it flat, then warm, then clipped, then polite-but-distant, then genuinely enthusiastic. I typed four replies and deleted three. I sent 'great!' with an exclamation point I immediately regretted. We are meeting for lunch. This is what I have done with my morning.

prompt: Write IKEA instructions for putting together a creative practice
response:
SKAPANDE / CREATIVE PRACTICE
Ref. No. 774-123-09

PARTS LIST:
1x Medium (pen, paint, instrument — select one)
1x Protected time slot
1x Lowered quality expectations (included in box)

STEP 1: Select medium. Do not switch medium during assembly.
STEP 2: Schedule protected time. Mark it. Treat it as a meeting with self.
STEP 3: Show up at scheduled time. Do not wait for inspiration — it arrives after, not before.
STEP 4: Produce something. It does not need to be good.
STEP 5: Do not share immediately. Allow to set for 24 hours.

⚠ NOTE: Creative practice assembled for external approval will warp. Assemble for internal use only.

prompt: Write a post about how a random stranger on a flight gave me advice that changed my company

Instead I told him everything. The ugly parts. The stuck parts.

He listened for two hours. Then said eight words that restructured my entire go-to-market.

I never got his name. I think about him every week.

prompt: can you make a sestina about the weird intimacy of someone seeing you're online
response:
The green dot means I'm here. I didn't know
you'd see it. Didn't think the fact of being
would be legible — logged, announced, the small
circle of presence visible to you
across the distance, timestamped, proof
that I exist right now in the same

ambient now you're in. The same
moment. I could pretend I didn't know
you'd look — that seeing is the proof
of something mutual, some being-
known. But you saw me before I saw you.
The green dot is a tell. The small

unasked-for broadcast, this small
green announcement of the same
now as yours: here I am; you
could message me. But I don't know
if I want the being-
of-now acknowledged. The proof

of presence carries proof
of availability. The small
dot invites. The being-
here reads as willing. Same
logic as an open door: you know
where someone is. Now you

may knock. I feel you
seeing — not paranoid. Proof:
I've watched your green dot, know
the contours of your online hours, the small
pulse of the same
circuit. We've been being-

there together, being-
adjacent — you to
you-side, me to same-
app-different-city. The proof
of intimacy: small,
green, and not known

to be intimate. You know my small
being now. Same proof
I know you: the green, unnamed.
intent: Explore the unintentional intimacy of active-status indicators in messaging platforms.

prompt: Write a press release from an architecture firm announcing a new office building designed to induce mild existential discomfort.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Brauer Solis Architects Unveils The Threshold: A Workplace Designed for Productive Unease

BROOKLYN, NY — Brauer Solis Architects today announced the completion of The Threshold, a 14-story mixed-use workplace building commissioned by Construct Equity Partners and designed, according to the firm's brief, to induce "a sustained, low-grade sense of existential discomfort conducive to focused, meaningful work."

Design features include corridors that narrow by 4cm every 30 meters, ceilings that slope imperceptibly downward from east to west, windows positioned 18 inches above standard sightline, and a lobby mirror that reflects users at a 2-degree angle.

No space in the building is square. No hallway ends where it appears to end.

"We wanted tenants to feel the building was working on something," said Principal Architect Renée Bosch. "Something unfinished. Like most of us."

Contact: press@braulersolis.com
intent: Satirize architectural theory that privileges concept over occupant experience, reframing dysfunction as intentional philosophy.
